course.summary: A hands-on, project-driven Rust course for developers who know Python, JavaScript, or Java. 9 modules, 27 lessons, one runnable mini-project per lesson — covering ownership through fearless concurrency.

modules.title: Setup & Rust Fundamentals
modules.strata_theme: T4: Canonical learning sequence validated across three independent sources
modules.description: Install Rust, write your first program, and learn the core building blocks: variables, types, functions, and control flow. Ends with a CLI temperature converter.
objectives: Set up a working Rust development environment with Cargo
Explain the difference between Rust's expressions and statements
Write functions with explicit type signatures and return values
Use match, loop, while, and for to control program flow

lessons.title: Installing Rust and Hello, Cargo
lessons.description: Install Rust via rustup, understand the role of Cargo, and write your first Rust program. Covers the project structure created by cargo new.
lessons.mini_project: hello_world — print a greeting, compile and run with cargo

lessons.title: Variables, Types, and Mutability
lessons.description: Rust variables are immutable by default. Learn scalar types (i32, f64, bool, char), compound types (tuples, arrays), shadowing, and the mut keyword.
lessons.mini_project: type_explorer — declare variables of 5 different types, shadow one, print all

lessons.title: Functions and Control Flow
lessons.description: Functions in Rust are expressions. Learn the -> return type syntax, if/else as an expression, loop/while/for, and ranges. Mini-project: CLI temperature converter.
lessons.mini_project: temperature_converter — CLI tool converting Fahrenheit ↔ Celsius ↔ Kelvin with match

modules.title: Ownership & Borrowing
modules.strata_theme: T1: Ownership is the central conceptual barrier; T3: Type system prevents bugs at compile time
modules.description: The single most important concept in Rust. Learn move semantics, borrowing rules, the borrow checker, and the String/&str distinction. This module is the gateway to everything else.
objectives: Explain Rust's ownership rules and why assignments are moves by default
Apply borrowing rules: when to use &T vs &mut T
Distinguish String (owned, heap) from &str (borrowed slice)
Read and understand borrow checker error messages

lessons.title: Ownership Rules and Move Semantics
lessons.description: Stack vs heap, the three ownership rules, why assignment is a move (not a copy), Copy vs Clone, and RAII (values dropped at end of scope). The bug this prevents: use-after-free and double-free.
lessons.mini_project: ownership_trace — write a program that demonstrates move, clone, and copy with printed output showing when values are dropped

lessons.title: References and the Borrow Checker
lessons.description: Borrowing as temporary access. The two rules: one &mut OR many &. Non-lexical lifetimes (NLL). Dangling reference prevention. The bug this prevents: data races and invalid memory access.
lessons.mini_project: borrow_checker_tour — write 3 functions: one takes &T, one takes &mut T, one takes T; call each and observe what can be used afterward

lessons.title: String vs &str and Slices
lessons.description: String owns heap data; &str borrows a slice. String operations (push_str, len, chars, split). The &String → &str deref coercion. When to use which in function parameters.
lessons.mini_project: string_reversal — reverse words in a sentence using &str slices, no String allocation in the processing function

modules.title: Structs & Methods
modules.strata_theme: T4: Canonical sequence — structs after ownership, before enums
modules.description: Group related data with structs. Add behavior with impl blocks. Use associated functions as constructors. Derive Debug and Clone.
objectives: Define structs and create instances using field init shorthand
Implement methods on structs using impl blocks
Write associated functions (constructors without self)
Derive common traits: Debug, Clone, PartialEq

lessons.title: Defining Structs
lessons.description: Struct syntax, field init shorthand, struct update syntax (..), tuple structs, unit structs. Ownership in structs: use owned types (String, not &str) until lifetimes are learned.
lessons.mini_project: point_struct — 2D Point struct with x, y fields; create instances using both explicit syntax and shorthand

lessons.title: Methods and impl Blocks
lessons.description: The impl block. Methods that take &self (read), &mut self (mutate), or self (consume). Method call auto-referencing. Multiple impl blocks.
lessons.mini_project: rectangle — Rectangle struct with area(), perimeter(), is_square() methods and new() constructor

lessons.title: Derived Traits and Debug
lessons.description: The #[derive] attribute for Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Copy. How {:?} and {:#?} format structs. When Copy is appropriate. Display trait with fmt::Display.
lessons.mini_project: student_grade_calculator — Student struct with name, grades Vec<f64>, average() method, Display impl, Debug derive

modules.title: Enums, Pattern Matching & Option
modules.strata_theme: T2: Enums replace three different language features; T3: Type system prevents null deref
modules.description: Rust enums carry data. match replaces switch and eliminates forgotten cases. Option<T> replaces null. Together, these three tools eliminate an entire category of runtime bugs.
objectives: Define enums with data-carrying variants
Write exhaustive match expressions including nested patterns
Use Option<T> in place of null — unwrap, if let, match
Apply pattern destructuring to structs, tuples, and enum variants

lessons.title: Enums and Data-Carrying Variants
lessons.description: Enum syntax, variants with no data / tuple data / struct data. Method on enums. Why enums with data eliminate the need for inheritance hierarchies.
lessons.mini_project: shape_enum — Shape enum with Circle(f64), Rectangle(f64, f64), Triangle(f64, f64, f64) variants and area() method

lessons.title: match and Pattern Matching
lessons.description: match is exhaustive — compiler enforces all cases. Patterns: literals, ranges, binding (x @ 1..=5), destructuring enums/structs/tuples, catch-all (_). if let as concise single-arm match.
lessons.mini_project: traffic_light — TrafficLight enum, match to get duration and instruction, if let for optional pedestrian signal

lessons.title: Option<T> — Rust's Null Alternative
lessons.description: Option<T> = Some(T) | None. Why there is no null in Rust. unwrap, expect, unwrap_or, map, and_then (Option chaining). The compiler forces you to handle None.
lessons.mini_project: contact_lookup — Vec<Contact> with find_by_name returning Option<&Contact>; chain map/unwrap_or for display

modules.title: Collections
modules.strata_theme: T4: Collections before error handling in validated sequence
modules.description: Vec<T>, HashMap<K,V>, and String operations. These three collections cover 80% of real-world Rust application data needs.
objectives: Create, populate, and iterate over Vec<T> using owned and borrowed patterns
Use HashMap with the entry API for safe insert-or-update
Perform common String operations: split, trim, chars, push_str
Choose the right collection type for a given use case

lessons.title: Vec<T> — Dynamic Arrays
lessons.description: Vec creation (vec![], Vec::new(), Vec::with_capacity), push/pop, indexing vs .get() (returns Option), slices, .iter()/.iter_mut()/.into_iter(), sorting.
lessons.mini_project: number_stats — Vec<f64> with functions for mean, median (sorted), min, max

lessons.title: HashMap<K, V>
lessons.description: HashMap creation, insert, get (returns Option<&V>), contains_key, remove, iteration. The entry API: .entry(key).or_insert(0) for safe count-or-create. HashMap ownership rules.
lessons.mini_project: word_frequency — count word occurrences in a hardcoded string using HashMap and entry API

lessons.title: String Operations
lessons.description: String is a Vec<u8> with UTF-8 guarantee. push_str, push (char), +, format!, len vs chars().count(), split, trim, contains, starts_with, to_uppercase/lowercase. Why String indexing with [] is disabled.
lessons.mini_project: word_frequency_complete — extend L14's word counter to normalize (trim, to_lowercase), print top-5 words sorted by count

modules.title: Error Handling
modules.strata_theme: T9: ? operator is idiomatic; T3: errors in type signature are documentation
modules.description: Rust makes errors part of the type signature. Learn the progression from match → expect → ? operator, and when to write custom error types.
objectives: Use Result<T, E> with match to handle recoverable errors explicitly
Apply the ? operator for ergonomic error propagation
Distinguish panic (unrecoverable) from Result (recoverable) errors
Write a custom error type implementing Display and std::error::Error

lessons.title: Result<T, E> and Recoverable Errors
lessons.description: Result<T, E> = Ok(T) | Err(E). match on Result. unwrap/expect (panic on Err). unwrap_or, map, map_err. panic! for unrecoverable states. When to panic vs return Err.
lessons.mini_project: safe_divide — fn divide(a: f64, b: f64) -> Result<f64, String>; caller handles Err with match

lessons.title: The ? Operator and Error Propagation
lessons.description: ? desugars to match + early return + From::from type conversion. Using ? in main with Box<dyn Error>. Chaining ? operators. The rule: ? can only be used in functions returning Result (or Option).
lessons.mini_project: file_reader — read a file, parse each line as f64, compute sum; all errors propagated with ?

lessons.title: Custom Error Types
lessons.description: When custom errors are needed (library code, multiple error kinds). enum AppError with variants. Implementing Display and std::error::Error. From<io::Error> for conversion with ?.
lessons.mini_project: csv_parser — parse CSV rows into structs with a ParseError enum; propagate IO and parse errors via ?

modules.title: Generics, Traits & Lifetimes
modules.strata_theme: T5: Lifetimes are annotation-light; T4: generics → traits → lifetimes ordering
modules.description: The Rust type system's most powerful tools. Generics let you write code once for all types. Traits define shared behavior. Lifetimes let the compiler verify memory assumptions.
objectives: Write generic functions and structs with <T> syntax
Define and implement traits with default methods
Use trait bounds in function signatures: impl Trait and where T: Trait
Apply lifetime annotations to functions where the compiler requires them

lessons.title: Generics
lessons.description: Generic functions, generic structs, generic enums (Option and Result are generic). Monomorphization: zero-cost abstraction — the compiler generates concrete types at compile time.
lessons.mini_project: generic_stack — Stack<T> with push, pop (returns Option<T>), peek, is_empty

lessons.title: Traits: Shared Behavior
lessons.description: Define traits, implement them on types, default method implementations. impl Trait parameter syntax vs where T: Trait bounds. Orphan rule. Blanket impls. Common stdlib traits: Display, Clone, Iterator, From/Into.
lessons.mini_project: shape_area_trait — Area trait with area() -> f64; implement for Circle, Rectangle, Triangle; generic fn print_area<T: Area + Display>(shape: &T)

lessons.title: Lifetimes
lessons.description: Lifetime elision rules first (most code needs no annotations). When explicit annotations are needed: multi-reference functions, structs holding references. Annotation syntax: 'a. The 'static lifetime. Common mistake: adding 'static to silence errors.
lessons.mini_project: longest_word — fn longest<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str; demonstrate lifetime error with dangling reference, then fix

modules.title: Closures & Iterators
modules.strata_theme: T6: Iterators are zero-cost and idiomatic over loops
modules.description: Closures are anonymous functions that capture their environment. Iterators are lazy sequences. Together they make functional programming idiomatic and zero-cost in Rust.
objectives: Write closures that capture variables by reference and by value (move)
Use the Fn, FnMut, FnOnce traits to understand closure types
Chain iterator adapters: map, filter, enumerate, flat_map
Collect iterator results into Vec, HashMap, and String

lessons.title: Closures
lessons.description: Closure syntax |x| x + 1. Environment capture: by reference (&), by mutable reference (&mut), by value (move). Fn/FnMut/FnOnce traits. Closures as function arguments (higher-order functions).
lessons.mini_project: sorter — sort Vec<String> by length using a closure; then by reverse alpha; demonstrate move closure capturing a threshold

lessons.title: The Iterator Trait
lessons.description: Iterator trait: only next() is required. Lazy evaluation: adaptors do nothing until consumed. iter() vs into_iter() vs iter_mut(). Consuming adaptors: sum, count, collect, fold. The for loop desugars to into_iter().
lessons.mini_project: vector_sum — sum, product, and max of a Vec<i32> using iterator consuming adaptors (no explicit loops)

lessons.title: Iterator Adapters and Chains
lessons.description: map, filter, enumerate, zip, take, skip, flat_map, chain. Collecting into Vec, HashMap, String. Idiomatic refactor: replace index loops with iterator chains. Zero-cost abstraction proof.
lessons.mini_project: log_analyzer — Vec<LogEntry { level: Level, message: String }>; filter by level, map to formatted strings, collect; second pass: rewrite an earlier loop as an iterator chain

modules.title: Concurrency
modules.strata_theme: T7: Concurrency safety is a direct consequence of ownership — fearless concurrency
modules.description: Rust's ownership rules prevent data races at compile time. Threads, message passing, and shared state — all enforced by the type system you already know.
objectives: Spawn threads with thread::spawn and collect results with JoinHandle
Transfer data to threads with move closures
Use mpsc channels for safe message passing between threads
Share state safely across threads with Arc<Mutex<T>>

lessons.title: Threads and JoinHandle
lessons.description: thread::spawn, JoinHandle::join, move closures for thread ownership transfer. Why the compiler requires move. The Send marker trait. Waiting for multiple threads.
lessons.mini_project: parallel_squares — spawn 4 threads, each computes squares of its chunk of numbers, join all, print results

lessons.title: Message Passing with Channels
lessons.description: mpsc::channel() returns (Sender<T>, Receiver<T>). Multiple producers, one consumer. Sender::send, Receiver::recv (blocking) and try_recv (non-blocking). Cloning Sender for multiple producers.
lessons.mini_project: pipeline — producer thread generates numbers, consumer thread receives and sums them; channel carries ownership

lessons.title: Shared State with Arc<Mutex<T>>
lessons.description: Rc<T> is not Send — why. Arc<T> is the thread-safe Rc. Mutex<T> for exclusive access at runtime. MutexGuard RAII release. Deadlock avoidance. The connection back to ownership: you already know this.
lessons.mini_project: parallel_word_counter — split a text into 4 chunks, spawn 4 threads, each counts words into a shared Arc<Mutex<HashMap>>; merge and print top-10

lessons.L01:
# Installing Rust and Hello, Cargo

**Module**: M01 · Setup & Rust Fundamentals  
**Type**: core  
**Estimated time**: 15 minutes  
**Claims**: C1 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

Every Rust project is managed by Cargo — the official build system and package manager. You don't compile Rust files manually; you use `cargo build`, `cargo run`, and `cargo test`. The first step is always `rustup`, which manages Rust toolchain versions.

## Setting up

Install Rust via [rustup.rs](https://rustup.rs). One command installs everything: `rustc` (the compiler), `cargo` (the build tool), and `rustup` (the toolchain manager).

```bash
# Install (macOS/Linux)
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh

# Verify
rustc --version
cargo --version
```

On Windows: download and run rustup-init.exe from rustup.rs.

## Your first project

```bash
cargo new hello_world
cd hello_world
```

This creates:
```
hello_world/
├── Cargo.toml   ← project manifest (name, version, dependencies)
└── src/
    └── main.rs  ← entry point
```

The default `src/main.rs`:
```rust
fn main() {
    println!("Hello, world!");
}
```

Run it:
```bash
cargo run
# Compiling hello_world v0.1.0
# Finished dev profile
# Hello, world!
```

## Key concepts

**println! is a macro**, not a function — the `!` is the tell. Macros are expanded at compile time and can accept variable argument counts, which regular functions can't.

**Cargo.toml** is your project's contract with the ecosystem:
```toml
[package]
name = "hello_world"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[dependencies]
# add crates here
```

**cargo commands you'll use daily:**
- `cargo new <name>` — create a project
- `cargo run` — compile and run
- `cargo build` — compile only (debug by default)
- `cargo build --release` — optimized build
- `cargo check` — type-check without producing a binary (fastest feedback loop)
- `cargo test` — run tests
- `cargo clippy` — run the Rust linter

## Why this matters

Unlike Python (`python script.py`) or JavaScript (`node script.js`), Rust is always compiled. There is no interpreter. `cargo run` compiles your entire project and runs the binary — errors are always caught before execution.

The compilation step is Rust's first line of defense.

---

## Mini-project: hello_world

Extend the default main.rs to:
1. Print a personalized greeting using a `name` variable
2. Print the Rust edition from a constant
3. Use `cargo check` to verify it compiles without running it

```rust
fn main() {
    let name = "Rustacean";
    const EDITION: u32 = 2021;
    println!("Hello, {}! Welcome to Rust Edition {}.", name, EDITION);
}
```

Run with `cargo run`. Notice the compilation output — this is what Rust does before every execution.
lessons.L02:
# Variables, Types, and Mutability

**Module**: M01 · Setup & Rust Fundamentals  
**Type**: core  
**Estimated time**: 18 minutes  
**Claims**: C1 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

Rust variables are immutable by default. This is a deliberate design choice: it makes code easier to reason about by making mutation explicit and intentional. When you see `let x = 5`, you know x will never change — unless it's `let mut x = 5`.

**Bug prevented here**: unintended mutation and shared mutable state.

## let and mut

```rust
let x = 5;        // immutable — x is always 5
x = 6;            // compile error: cannot assign twice to immutable variable

let mut y = 5;    // mutable — y can change
y = 6;            // fine
```

## Shadowing

Shadowing is not mutation — it creates a new binding:

```rust
let x = 5;
let x = x + 1;     // new binding — x is now 6
let x = x * 2;     // new binding — x is now 12
println!("{x}");   // 12

let spaces = "   ";          // &str
let spaces = spaces.len();   // usize — shadowing can change the type
```

Key difference from mut: shadowing can change the type; mutation cannot.

## Scalar types

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| `i32` | 32-bit signed integer (default) | `let n: i32 = -5;` |
| `u64` | 64-bit unsigned integer | `let big: u64 = 1_000_000;` |
| `f64` | 64-bit float (default) | `let pi = 3.14159;` |
| `bool` | true or false | `let flag = true;` |
| `char` | Unicode scalar value | `let c = 'Z';` |

Rust requires explicit casts between numeric types:
```rust
let x: i32 = 5;
let y: f64 = x as f64;   // explicit cast
```

## Compound types

**Tuple**: fixed-length, heterogeneous:
```rust
let tup: (i32, f64, bool) = (500, 6.4, true);
let (x, y, z) = tup;           // destructuring
println!("{}", tup.0);         // index access
```

**Array**: fixed-length, homogeneous (stack-allocated):
```rust
let a = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
let b = [0; 5];                // [0, 0, 0, 0, 0]
println!("{}", a[0]);          // index access
```

Arrays are not growable — use Vec<T> for dynamic arrays (Module 5).

## Constants

Constants (`const`) are always immutable, require a type annotation, and can be declared in any scope:

```rust
const MAX_SCORE: u32 = 100_000;
```

Use `1_000_000` not `1000000` for readability — underscores in numeric literals are ignored.

---

## Mini-project: type_explorer

```rust
fn main() {
    // Scalar types
    let age: u8 = 30;
    let temperature: f64 = 98.6;
    let is_active: bool = true;
    let grade: char = 'A';

    // Shadowing — transform without mut
    let input = "42";
    let input: i32 = input.parse().expect("not a number");

    // Tuple destructuring
    let coords = (40.7128_f64, -74.0060_f64);
    let (lat, lon) = coords;

    // Constant
    const VERSION: &str = "1.0.0";

    println!("Age: {age}, Temp: {temperature}, Active: {is_active}, Grade: {grade}");
    println!("Parsed: {input}");
    println!("Lat: {lat}, Lon: {lon}");
    println!("Version: {VERSION}");
}
```

Verify: `cargo run` — all values printed. Then try changing `let age` to `age = 31` and observe the compile error.

cards.front: What is Cargo, and what does it do?
cards.back: Cargo is Rust's official build system and package manager. It handles: compiling your code (cargo build), running it (cargo run), running tests (cargo test), managing dependencies in Cargo.toml, and publishing packages to crates.io.

cards.front: In Rust, variables are {{immutable}} by default. To allow mutation, you add the {{mut}} keyword.
cards.back: Variables are immutable by default. Use `let mut x = 5;` to make x mutable. This forces intentional mutation and makes code easier to reason about.

cards.front: What is the difference between shadowing and mut in Rust?
cards.back: Shadowing (`let x = x + 1;`) creates a new binding and can change the type. mut allows reassigning the same binding but not changing the type. Shadowing is preferred when transforming a value into a different type.

cards.front: In Rust, if/else is an {{expression}}, not a statement — the last expression in each arm is the {{return value}}.
cards.back: Example: `let result = if condition { 5 } else { 6 };` — both arms must have the same type.

cards.front: What are Rust's three ownership rules?
cards.back: 1. Each value has exactly one owner. 2. There can only be one owner at a time. 3. When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped (RAII). These three rules together eliminate memory leaks, use-after-free, and double-free bugs.

cards.front: When you assign `s2 = s1` where s1 is a String, Rust {{moves}} the ownership. After the move, using s1 is a {{compile error}}.
cards.back: Move semantics mean only s2 owns the String data. If you need both, use `s2 = s1.clone()`. Types that implement Copy (like i32) are copied instead of moved.

cards.front: Why does Rust use move semantics instead of automatic deep copy?
cards.back: Deep copy is expensive for heap data. By making moves the default, Rust forces programmers to explicitly choose copying with .clone() — making performance costs visible. Types that are cheap to copy (i32, bool, f64) implement Copy and are copied automatically.

cards.front: What are Rust's two borrowing rules?
cards.back: 1. At any time, you may have either one mutable reference (&mut T) OR any number of immutable references (&T) — never both. 2. References must always be valid (no dangling references). These rules are enforced at compile time by the borrow checker.

cards.front: Rust prevents {{data races}} at compile time. A data race requires two concurrent accesses where at least one is a {{write}} and there is no synchronization.
cards.back: Because Rust forbids simultaneous &mut T and &T references, a data race is structurally impossible in safe Rust — the borrow checker rejects the code that would allow it.

cards.front: What is the difference between String and &str in Rust?
cards.back: String: heap-allocated, owned, growable sequence of UTF-8 bytes. &str: an immutable borrowed reference to a sequence of UTF-8 bytes (a 'slice'). String literals like "hello" have type &str and are stored in the binary. Use &str in function parameters when you don't need ownership.

cards.front: Prefer {{&str}} over {{&String}} in function parameters because of Rust's deref coercion — &String automatically coerces to &str.
cards.back: fn greet(name: &str) accepts both &str and &String callers. fn greet(name: &String) only accepts &String. The &str version is more flexible and idiomatic.

cards.front: What is struct update syntax in Rust?
cards.back: Struct update syntax `Point { x: 1.0, ..other }` creates a new struct with some fields from another instance. The `..` copies remaining fields from `other`. Note: if any moved field is not Copy, the original is partially moved.

cards.front: In a Rust impl block, methods take {{&self}}, {{&mut self}}, or {{self}} as the first parameter, corresponding to borrow, mutable borrow, or consume.
cards.back: The method call `rect.area()` is sugar for `Rectangle::area(&rect)` — Rust automatically references/dereferences to match the method signature.

cards.front: What is the difference between a method and an associated function in Rust?
cards.back: Methods take self as the first parameter and are called with dot syntax (instance.method()). Associated functions don't take self and are called with :: syntax (Type::function()). Associated functions are typically used as constructors: `Rectangle::new(width, height)`.

cards.front: Why should you prefer owned types (String) over references (&str) in structs, especially early in learning Rust?
cards.back: Storing references in structs requires lifetime annotations — the compiler needs to know the borrowed data lives at least as long as the struct. Using owned types (String instead of &str) avoids this requirement entirely. Prefer owned types in structs until lifetimes are well understood.

cards.front: How do Rust enums differ from enums in Java or C?
cards.back: Rust enums can have data attached to each variant. Example: `enum Message { Quit, Move { x: i32, y: i32 }, Write(String), ChangeColor(i32, i32, i32) }`. Each variant can have different data types and amounts. This eliminates the need for class hierarchies for sum types.

cards.front: Rust's match is {{exhaustive}} — the compiler ensures all possible variants are {{handled}}. This prevents forgotten cases at compile time, not at runtime.
cards.back: If you add a new variant to an enum, every match expression on that enum becomes a compile error until you handle the new variant. The `_` pattern acts as a catch-all.

cards.front: What is Option<T> and why does Rust use it instead of null?
cards.back: Option<T> is an enum: `Some(T)` (contains a value) or `None` (no value). The type system forces you to handle both cases — you cannot use an Option<T> as if it were T without unwrapping it. This eliminates null pointer exceptions at compile time. Rust has no null value.

cards.front: `Option::map` applies a function to the inner value if {{Some}}, and returns {{None}} if the option is None — without unwrapping.
cards.back: Example: `Some(5).map(|x| x * 2)` = `Some(10)`. `None.map(|x: i32| x * 2)` = `None`. Chain with `and_then` for operations that also return Option.

cards.front: Why should you use .get(index) instead of v[index] when accessing Vec elements?
cards.back: .get(index) returns Option<&T> — Some(&value) if in bounds, None if out of bounds. v[index] panics if out of bounds. Use .get() when the index might be invalid; use [] only when you're certain the index is valid (e.g., within a loop bounded by v.len()).

cards.front: The HashMap entry API `map.entry(key).or_insert({{0}})` safely inserts a default value if the key is absent, and returns a {{mutable reference}} to the value either way.
cards.back: Example for counting: `*map.entry(word).or_insert(0) += 1;` — the `*` dereferences the &mut i32 to increment it. This is more efficient than checking contains_key and then inserting.

cards.front: Why can't you index into a String with `s[0]` in Rust?
cards.back: String is a UTF-8 encoded sequence. A single char may be 1–4 bytes. Index 0 would be the first byte, not the first character — which could be the middle of a multi-byte character, producing invalid data. Rust prevents this at compile time. Use .chars().nth(0) for character access.

cards.front: What is the difference between panic! and Result in Rust error handling?
cards.back: panic! is for unrecoverable errors — states that should never happen (programmer bugs, invariant violations). It unwinds the stack and terminates. Result<T, E> is for recoverable errors — expected failure modes the caller should handle (file not found, parse failure, network timeout). Use Result at API boundaries; use panic! only for bugs.

cards.front: The `?` operator in Rust desugars to: if Err, {{return Err(e.into())}}; if Ok, {{unwrap and continue}}.
cards.back: The `.into()` call invokes From::from, converting the error type automatically. This means ? works across different error types as long as From implementations exist. ? can only be used in functions that return Result (or Option).

cards.front: When should you use Box<dyn Error> as the return type instead of a specific error type?
cards.back: Use Box<dyn Error> in main() and in binary code where you want to propagate any error type without defining a custom enum. In library code, prefer a concrete error type or a custom enum so callers can pattern-match on specific errors. Box<dyn Error> trades specificity for ergonomics.

cards.front: A custom error type needs to implement {{Display}} (for human-readable messages) and {{std::error::Error}} (for the error trait object infrastructure).
cards.back: Minimal impl: `impl Display for AppError { fn fmt(...) }` + `impl std::error::Error for AppError {}`. The Error trait has no required methods — the default implementation is sufficient for most cases.

cards.front: What is monomorphization in Rust generics?
cards.back: Monomorphization is the compile-time process of creating concrete implementations of generic code for each type it's used with. `fn largest<T>(list: &[T])` called with &[i32] and &[f64] produces two compiled functions. This makes generics zero-cost — no runtime overhead from using generics.

cards.front: The {{orphan rule}} in Rust states: you can implement a trait on a type only if either the {{trait}} or the {{type}} is defined in your crate.
cards.back: You CAN implement Display for your own struct. You CAN implement your own trait for String. You CANNOT implement Display (foreign trait) for String (foreign type) — this prevents conflicting implementations.

cards.front: What is the difference between impl Trait parameter syntax and a where clause?
cards.back: Both express trait bounds. `fn f(x: impl Display)` = `fn f<T: Display>(x: T)`. Use where for: multiple bounds (`T: Display + Clone`), when the signature would be unreadable inline, or when the same type parameter appears multiple times. Both are equivalent to the compiler.

cards.front: Lifetime annotations don't {{change}} how long references live — they describe {{relationships}} between lifetimes so the compiler can verify them.
cards.back: In `fn longest<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str`, the 'a means: the return lives at least as long as the shorter of x and y. The annotation doesn't extend anyone's lifetime.

cards.front: What are Rust's three lifetime elision rules?
cards.back: 1. Each reference parameter gets its own lifetime parameter. 2. If there is exactly one input lifetime, that lifetime is assigned to all output lifetimes. 3. If one of the input lifetimes is &self or &mut self, the lifetime of self is assigned to all output lifetimes. If all outputs are resolved after these rules, no explicit annotations are needed.

cards.front: When is the 'static lifetime appropriate, and what is the most common misuse?
cards.back: 'static is appropriate for string literals (which live in the binary) and for data that genuinely must outlive all other references. Common misuse: adding 'static to silence lifetime errors. This almost always indicates a design problem — use owned data (String instead of &str) or restructure ownership rather than demanding 'static.

cards.front: What is the difference between Fn, FnMut, and FnOnce closure traits?
cards.back: FnOnce: closure can only be called once — it takes ownership of captured values. FnMut: closure can be called multiple times and may mutate captured values. Fn: closure can be called multiple times without mutating captured values (most common). Every closure implements at least FnOnce; those that don't consume captures also implement FnMut; those that don't mutate also implement Fn.

cards.front: Iterators in Rust are {{lazy}} — iterator adapters like map() and filter() do nothing until a {{consuming}} adapter is called.
cards.back: Example: `v.iter().map(|x| x + 1)` creates an iterator but computes nothing. Only when `.collect()`, `.sum()`, or a for loop consumes it does any work happen.

cards.front: What is the difference between iter(), into_iter(), and iter_mut() on a Vec?
cards.back: iter() yields &T — immutable references, Vec is not consumed. iter_mut() yields &mut T — mutable references, Vec is not consumed. into_iter() yields T — owned values, Vec is consumed (moved). Use iter() when you want to read elements, into_iter() when you want to transform them into something new.

cards.front: Rewrite this loop as an iterator chain: `let mut result = Vec::new(); for x in &v { if x % 2 == 0 { result.push(x * x); } }`
cards.back: `let result: Vec<_> = v.iter().filter(|x| *x % 2 == 0).map(|x| x * x).collect();` — the iterator chain is more readable, compile-equivalent in performance, and eliminates the mutable accumulator.

cards.front: Iterator chains in Rust are a {{zero-cost abstraction}} — they compile to the same machine code as hand-written {{for loops}}.
cards.back: The Rust compiler (via LLVM) unrolls and inlines iterator chains. You get the readability of functional style with the performance of explicit loops. There is no runtime overhead from chaining adapters.

cards.front: What is the Send trait in Rust, and why is it required for thread::spawn?
cards.back: Send is a marker trait that indicates a type is safe to transfer to another thread. thread::spawn requires the closure to be Send because the closure and any captured values cross a thread boundary. Rc<T> is not Send (reference counts are not atomic); Arc<T> is Send. The compiler enforces this — you cannot accidentally share non-thread-safe types across threads.

cards.front: Thread closures in Rust typically use the {{move}} keyword to transfer {{ownership}} of captured variables to the spawned thread.
cards.back: Without move, the closure captures by reference — but the thread might outlive the reference's validity. move forces ownership transfer, making the reference valid for the thread's entire lifetime.

cards.front: When should you use channels vs Arc<Mutex<T>> for concurrency in Rust?
cards.back: Channels (mpsc) for message passing: prefer when threads have clear producer/consumer roles and data ownership transfers between threads. Arc<Mutex<T>> for shared state: prefer when multiple threads need to read and write the same data without passing ownership. Channels avoid the risk of deadlock; Mutex requires careful lock ordering to avoid deadlock.

cards.front: `Arc<T>` is the thread-safe version of `Rc<T>`. Arc uses {{atomic}} reference counting; Rc uses {{non-atomic}} reference counting. Only Arc implements {{Send}}.
cards.back: Use Rc<T> for single-threaded scenarios (more performant). Use Arc<T> when sharing ownership across threads. Combine Arc with Mutex for mutable shared state: Arc<Mutex<T>>.

cards.front: Why is Rust's concurrency called 'fearless concurrency'?
cards.back: The same ownership and type system that prevents memory bugs also prevents data races. Rc<T> isn't Send (compile error if you try to share across threads). &mut T can't be shared (borrow checker prevents simultaneous writes). Arc<Mutex<T>> enforces exclusive access at the type level. Data races are compile-time errors, not runtime races you need a profiler to find.

cards.front: What does the evidence say about whether ownership is just a 'feature' in Rust?
cards.back: Ownership is not a feature — it is the organizing principle of the entire language. Lifetimes, traits, Option<T>, iterators, and concurrency all derive their safety guarantees from ownership rules. Confidence: high. Sources: S001, S002, S005, S010.

cards.front: What does the evidence say about teaching enums, Option<T>, and match as separate topics?
cards.back: Teaching them separately is pedagogically inferior. Option<T> loses meaning without match; match loses motivation without data-carrying enums. The 'no null in Rust' insight is the anchor — teach all three together in one module. Confidence: high. Source: S009.

cards.front: What does the evidence say about how often explicit lifetime annotations are needed in practice?
cards.back: Lifetime elision rules cover 80–90% of real Rust code. Most method signatures never need explicit annotations (elision rule 3 handles &self methods). Explicit 'a annotations appear mainly in library code with multi-reference functions. Confidence: medium-high. Source: S010.

cards.front: In Rust, `cargo {{new}} project_name` creates a new project; `cargo {{run}}` compiles and executes it.
cards.back: cargo new creates Cargo.toml (manifest) and src/main.rs. cargo run is equivalent to cargo build && ./target/debug/project_name but more ergonomic.

cards.front: What does Result<T, E> represent in Rust?
cards.back: Result<T, E> is an enum with two variants: Ok(T) for success (carrying the value), Err(E) for failure (carrying the error). It is how Rust encodes fallible operations at the type level — the caller is forced to handle both cases. Common in I/O, parsing, and any operation that can fail for external reasons.

cards.front: The `if let Some(x) = option` syntax is syntactic sugar for a {{match}} with one arm; it's preferred when you only care about the {{Some}} case.
cards.back: Equivalent: `match option { Some(x) => { /* use x */ }, _ => {} }`. Use if let when the None case needs no action; use match when you need to handle None explicitly.

cards.front: Why do thread closures often need `move` but regular closures usually don't?
cards.back: A spawned thread may outlive the scope where the closure was created. If the closure captured by reference, the reference could become invalid (dangling) after the original scope ends. `move` transfers ownership of captured values into the closure, ensuring they live as long as the thread. Regular closures (passed to map, filter) are called immediately in the same scope, so references remain valid.

cards.front: NLL (Non-Lexical Lifetimes) means a borrow ends at its {{last use}}, not at the end of the {{enclosing block}}.
cards.back: Before NLL, this would fail: `let mut s = String::from("hi"); let r = &s; println!("{r}"); s.push_str(" there");` — NLL sees that r is last used before the push_str, so the borrow is already released. NLL makes many valid programs compile that older Rust would reject.

cards.front: What does mpsc stand for and how does the Rust channel work?
cards.back: mpsc = Multiple Producer, Single Consumer. `std::sync::mpsc::channel()` returns `(Sender<T>, Receiver<T>)`. Multiple Sender instances (cloned with .clone()) can send values to one Receiver. Sender::send() transfers ownership of the value. Receiver::recv() blocks until a value arrives or all Senders are dropped.

cards.front: A {{blanket implementation}} implements a trait for any type satisfying a bound. Example: `impl<T: Display> Printable for T` implements Printable for {{every type that implements Display}}.
cards.back: The Rust stdlib uses this: `impl<T: Display> ToString for T {}` — that's why any type implementing Display automatically gets a .to_string() method.

cards.front: What happens to a Vec's elements when you call into_iter() vs iter()?
cards.back: into_iter() consumes the Vec — each element is moved out (owned T). After into_iter(), you cannot use the Vec again. iter() borrows the Vec — each element is an immutable reference (&T). The Vec is still valid after iteration. Use iter() to inspect; use into_iter() to transform into a new collection.

cards.front: The `#[derive(Debug)]` attribute automatically implements the {{Debug}} trait, enabling `{:?}` and `{:#?}` ({{pretty-print}}) format specifiers.
cards.back: You can only derive Debug if all fields also implement Debug. For custom formatting, implement fmt::Display instead (which enables `{}`). They serve different audiences: Debug for developers, Display for end users.

cards.front: What is the difference between unwrap() and expect() on a Result?
cards.back: Both panic on Err. unwrap() panics with a generic message: 'called Result::unwrap() on an Err value: ...' — hard to debug. expect("message") panics with your custom message — much more useful in a stack trace. Prefer expect() over unwrap() whenever you must use either; prefer ? over both in production code.

cards.front: `Mutex::lock()` returns a `MutexGuard<T>`. The lock is {{automatically released}} when the guard {{goes out of scope}} (RAII).
cards.back: You do not need to explicitly unlock. This prevents lock leaks. Use `drop(guard)` to release early if needed. Deadlock occurs if a thread calls lock() while already holding the lock — Rust does not prevent this at compile time.

cards.front: What does RAII mean in Rust, and why is it important?
cards.back: RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization): resources (heap memory, file handles, locks) are tied to the lifetime of a value. When the value goes out of scope, Rust automatically calls drop(), freeing the resource. No manual free(), no garbage collector. This makes Rust's memory management deterministic and leak-free in safe code.

cards.front: The `match` expression in Rust must be {{exhaustive}} — if you don't handle all variants, the code {{won't compile}}.
cards.back: This is a compile-time guarantee, not a runtime check. If you add a new variant to an enum later, every match on that enum becomes a compile error — forcing you to handle it. Use `_` as a catch-all only when you genuinely don't care about the remaining cases.

cards.front: What is the Iterator trait's only required method?
cards.back: `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>` — returns Some(item) while items remain, then None when exhausted. All other iterator methods (map, filter, sum, collect, etc.) are provided as default implementations built on top of next. Implementing only next() gives you access to the entire iterator ecosystem.

cards.front: Generic code in Rust is {{monomorphized}} at compile time — one generic function produces {{multiple concrete functions}}, one per type it's called with.
cards.back: This is why Rust generics are zero-cost: no dynamic dispatch, no virtual method tables. The trade-off is longer compile times and larger binaries (each instantiation is compiled separately).

cards.front: When is it worth defining a custom error enum instead of using Box<dyn Error>?
cards.back: Use a custom error enum when: (1) callers need to match on specific error variants, (2) you are writing a library (type erasure in Box<dyn Error> removes caller flexibility), (3) you have multiple distinct failure modes that warrant different handling. For application main() or quick scripts, Box<dyn Error> is fine.

cards.front: HashMap's {{get}} method returns `Option<{{&V}}>` — Some(&value) if found, None if absent.
cards.back: Always returns a reference (borrow), not the owned value — you'd need remove() to take ownership. This ensures the HashMap retains ownership of its values while allowing read access.

cards.front: Which types implement Copy in Rust, and which must be cloned?
cards.back: Copy types (assignment copies, not moves): all integer types (i32, u64...), floating point (f64), bool, char, tuples/arrays of Copy types, references &T. Must be cloned: String, Vec<T>, HashMap, Box<T>, any heap-allocated type. The rule: if the type owns heap memory or a resource, it cannot be Copy.

cards.front: `.collect::<Vec<_>>()` or `.collect::<HashMap<_, _>>()` — the type annotation is needed because collect can produce {{many different collection types}} and Rust needs a hint to resolve the {{ambiguity}}.
cards.back: You can provide the type on the left: `let v: Vec<_> = iter.collect();` or inline: `iter.collect::<Vec<_>>()`. The `_` lets Rust infer the element type.

cards.front: When should you take &mut self vs self in a method?
cards.back: &self: read-only access, can be called on owned values and references. &mut self: modify the instance, caller must have mutable binding. self: consume the instance (useful for builder pattern or conversion methods). Taking self is rare — it prevents the caller from using the value again. Default to &self; use &mut self when mutation is needed.

cards.front: In Rust, a function returns the value of its {{last expression}} (no semicolon). Adding a semicolon turns it into a {{statement}} that returns ().
What is the purpose of the format! macro vs String::from and +?
cards.back: format!("{name} is {age}") creates a new String by interpolating values — it doesn't take ownership of any argument. The + operator requires the left side to be an owned String and borrows the right side (&str) — messy for multi-string concatenation. Use format! for readable, ownership-safe string building; use push_str for appending in a loop.

cards.front: Lifetime elision rule 3: if one of the inputs is {{&self}} or {{&mut self}}, the lifetime of self is assigned to all output lifetimes — this is why most struct method return types need no lifetime annotation.
cards.back: Example: `fn first_word(&self) -> &str` — the compiler infers the return &str borrows from self. No 'a annotation needed.

cards.front: Compare how Python and Rust handle the absence of a value in a dictionary lookup.
cards.back: Python dict.get(key) returns None (untyped — the same None used everywhere, no compile-time guarantee). Rust HashMap::get(key) returns Option<&V> — the type system enforces that you handle both Some(&v) and None before using the value. A Python None isn't guaranteed to cause an error until it propagates; Rust's Option<T> is a compile-time guarantee the caller must address.

cards.front: JoinHandle::{{join}}() waits for the spawned thread to finish and returns `Result<T, Box<dyn Any>>` where T is the thread's {{return value}}.
cards.back: If the thread panicked, join() returns Err. Calling join() on all spawned threads before exiting main prevents the program from killing running threads prematurely.

cards.front: What does the From trait have to do with the ? operator?
cards.back: When ? encounters an Err(e), it calls From::from(e) to convert e into the function's declared error type. This allows ? to work across different error types automatically — as long as From<source_error_type> is implemented for the function's error type. For Box<dyn Error>, all error types that implement Error are automatically convertible.

cards.front: A {{move}} closure takes {{ownership}} of its captured variables, making it useful for spawning threads or returning closures from functions.
cards.back: Without move: closure captures by reference, requiring the referent to outlive the closure. With move: closure owns its captures, so it can outlive the original scope. The thread::spawn closure almost always requires move.

cards.front: A type implements Display. Does it automatically implement ToString?
cards.back: Yes — via a blanket implementation in the Rust standard library: `impl<T: fmt::Display> ToString for T`. This is the standard way Rust provides method access based on trait bounds without requiring manual implementation for every type. The .to_string() method is available on any type implementing Display.

cards.front: A {{tuple struct}} like `struct Color(i32, i32, i32)` is accessed by {{index}}: `color.0`, `color.1`, `color.2`.
cards.back: Tuple structs are useful when you want a distinct named type but don't need field names. Unlike plain tuples, `Color(255, 0, 0)` and `Point(255, 0, 0)` are different types even though they have the same fields.

cards.front: Why is Rc<T> not thread-safe, and how does Arc<T> solve this?
cards.back: Rc<T> uses non-atomic reference counting — incrementing and decrementing the count is not thread-safe (a data race on the count itself). Arc<T> uses atomic operations for reference counting, ensuring correctness when cloned across threads. The name Arc = Atomically Reference Counted. Arc has slightly higher overhead; only use it when threads are involved.

cards.front: Calling `.unwrap()` on `Err(e)` causes a {{panic}} with the message: "called Result::unwrap() on an Err value: {{e}}".
cards.back: unwrap() is useful during prototyping or in tests where you expect success. In production code, use expect("descriptive message") for better error messages, or ? for proper propagation.

cards.front: What is the risk of using v[i] vs v.get(i) when iterating with an index in Rust?
cards.back: v[i] panics at runtime with 'index out of bounds' if i >= v.len(). v.get(i) returns None safely. When iterating with a range, ensure the range is bounded by v.len() — or better, avoid index-based iteration entirely and use iterators (for item in &v) which are always safe.

cards.front: You can have {{many}} immutable references (&T) to a value OR {{one}} mutable reference (&mut T), but never {{both}} at the same time.
cards.back: This is enforced at compile time by the borrow checker. The constraint enables the compiler to guarantee freedom from data races and aliasing bugs without any runtime cost.

cards.front: What does enumerate() return, and when is it useful?
cards.back: enumerate() wraps an iterator to produce (index, value) pairs: `Iterator<Item = (usize, T)>`. Use it when you need both the element and its position: `for (i, item) in v.iter().enumerate()`. This replaces the classic `for i in 0..v.len()` index loop with a safe, idiomatic alternative.

cards.front: After `tx.send(value)`, the {{ownership}} of `value` is transferred to the {{receiver}}. This is why channels enable safe data transfer: no aliasing possible.
cards.back: The Sender sends T (owned). The Receiver receives T (owned). The borrow checker ensures the sender has released its ownership before the receiver can use the value. This is the channel's safety guarantee.

cards.front: Write the signature for a generic function that takes a slice of any type and returns the largest element, requiring the element type to be comparable.
cards.back: `fn largest<T: PartialOrd>(list: &[T]) -> &T` — the PartialOrd trait bound enables the > operator. The function returns a reference to avoid moving elements out of the slice. This is one of the first examples in the Rust Book for generics with trait bounds.

questions.question: What does the command `cargo new my_project` produce?

options.A: A single main.rs file in the current directory
options.B: A directory with src/main.rs and Cargo.toml
options.C: A compiled binary called my_project
options.D: A Cargo.toml with an empty dependencies section only

questions.explanation: cargo new creates a named project directory containing src/main.rs (a hello-world entry point) and Cargo.toml (the manifest). It also initialises a git repository by default.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly demonstrates variable shadowing in Rust?

options.A: let mut x = 5; x = x + 1;
options.B: let x = 5; let x = x + 1;
options.C: var x = 5; var x = x + 1;
options.D: const x = 5; x = x + 1;

questions.explanation: Shadowing re-declares the same name with `let`. Option A uses mutation (mut), not shadowing. Options C and D are not valid Rust syntax.

questions.question: What is the type of the tuple `(1, 'a', true)` in Rust?

options.A: tuple
options.B: (i32, char, bool)
options.C: [i32, char, bool]
options.D: Vec<any>

questions.explanation: Rust tuple types are written as a parenthesised list of the element types: (i32, char, bool). Option C is array syntax, which requires a single element type.

options.A: Nothing — there is no return statement
options.B: A compile error — the return keyword is missing
options.C: x + y — the last expression without a semicolon is the implicit return
options.D: 0 — Rust initialises integers to zero by default

questions.explanation: Rust functions return the value of the last expression when there is no trailing semicolon. The explicit `return` keyword is optional and typically only used for early returns.

questions.question: Which loop construct is the only one that can return a value from a loop body?

options.A: while
options.B: for
options.C: loop
options.D: All loop types can return values

questions.explanation: `loop` is Rust's infinite loop and the only one that can return a value via `break value`. `while` and `for` loops always evaluate to `()` (unit).

questions.question: What is the key difference between `mut` and shadowing when changing a variable's value?

options.A: mut is faster; shadowing is safer
options.B: Shadowing can change the type; mut cannot
options.C: mut only works on integers; shadowing works on all types
options.D: There is no practical difference

questions.explanation: Shadowing with `let` creates a new binding, so you can change the type (e.g., from &str to usize). `mut` only lets you change the value — the type is fixed at first binding.

questions.question: Explain the difference between an expression and a statement in Rust. Give one example of each.

required_points: Expressions evaluate to a value
Statements perform an action and do not return a value (they return unit ())
A let binding is a statement; an arithmetic operation like x + 1 is an expression
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Any valid Rust expression and statement pair is acceptable.
answer_key.full_credit: Expressions return a value (e.g., 5 + 3 evaluates to 8). Statements perform an action but return () — e.g., `let x = 5;` is a statement and cannot be used on the right-hand side of another let.
answer_key.partial_credit: Correctly defines one of the two terms without the other.

questions.question: You have a variable `let x = 5;`. Without changing this declaration, write two lines of Rust that shadow it with the value 10 and then print it.

required_points: Uses `let x = 10;` to shadow (not `x = 10;` which would be a mutation error)
Uses println! to print x
answer_key.acceptable_variations: `let x = x * 2;` is also valid shadowing since 5 * 2 = 10.
answer_key.full_credit: `let x = 10; println!("{}", x);` or any expression that produces 10 via shadowing.
answer_key.partial_credit: Correctly describes the concept but has minor syntax errors.

questions.prompt: You are helping a Python developer learn Rust. They are confused because their first Rust variable is immutable by default. Explain Rust's design decision to make variables immutable by default, how shadowing provides a safe alternative to mutation, and one situation where `mut` is genuinely the right choice.
questions.guidance: A strong response covers: (1) safety rationale — prevents accidental mutation; (2) shadowing as a way to transform values without mutation; (3) a concrete use case for mut such as a loop counter or accumulator.

rubric.A: Explains the safety rationale, demonstrates shadowing with a type change, and gives a concrete justified use case for mut.
rubric.B: Explains immutability and shadowing correctly but the mut use case is vague.
rubric.C: Mentions immutability but confuses shadowing with mutation.

options.A: Both s1 and s2 are valid String owners after the assignment
options.B: s1 is copied; both variables are valid and independent
options.C: s1 is moved into s2; s1 is no longer valid
options.D: s2 borrows s1; s1 is still the owner

questions.explanation: String does not implement Copy, so assignment moves ownership to s2. After the move, s1 is no longer valid and using it is a compile error. This is the ownership rule: each value has exactly one owner.

questions.question: Which of the following types implements the `Copy` trait?

options.A: String
options.B: Vec<i32>
options.D: Box<i32>

questions.explanation: i32 is a fixed-size scalar stored on the stack, so it implements Copy. String, Vec, and Box own heap data and implement Drop instead of Copy — these are always moved.

questions.question: How many mutable references to the same value are allowed to exist simultaneously in Rust?

options.A: Unlimited, as long as they are in different threads
options.B: Two — one reader, one writer
options.C: One
options.D: Unlimited, as long as they are in the same scope

questions.explanation: Rust's borrow rule: you may have EITHER one mutable reference OR any number of immutable references, but not both at the same time. This prevents data races at compile time.

questions.question:
What does this code demonstrate?

```rust
fn first_word(s: &str) -> &str { ... }
```
Why is `&str` preferred over `&String` as the parameter type?

options.A: &str is faster to pass than &String
options.B: &String cannot be passed to functions
options.C: &str accepts both string literals and &String via deref coercion, making the function more flexible
options.D: &str owns the data; &String does not

questions.explanation: A function taking &str can be called with a string literal (&'static str) or with &my_string (Deref coercion converts &String to &str automatically). Taking &String restricts callers unnecessarily.

questions.question: What bug does Rust's borrow checker prevent that C and C++ do not?

options.A: Integer overflow
options.B: Using a reference to data that has already been freed (use-after-free)
options.C: Dividing by zero
options.D: Stack overflow from infinite recursion

questions.explanation: The borrow checker tracks lifetimes and ensures references never outlive the data they point to. This eliminates use-after-free and dangling pointer bugs entirely at compile time.

questions.question: When is RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) executed for a Rust value?

options.A: When the garbage collector runs
options.B: When the programmer calls free() or delete
options.C: When the value goes out of scope — the Drop trait is called automatically
options.D: Only when the program exits

questions.explanation: Rust calls the Drop trait implementation (equivalent to a destructor) automatically when a value leaves its scope. There is no garbage collector and no manual free() — the compiler inserts the cleanup at the exact right point.

questions.question: State Rust's three ownership rules and explain why they collectively prevent double-free errors.

required_points: Rule 1: Each value in Rust has exactly one owner
Rule 2: There can only be one owner at a time
Rule 3: When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped
Double-free is prevented because only one owner can trigger Drop
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Paraphrasing of the rules is acceptable as long as all three are present.
answer_key.full_credit: All three rules stated correctly with an explanation that single ownership means Drop is called exactly once.
answer_key.partial_credit: Two of three rules correctly stated, or all three without the double-free connection.

questions.question: You write a function `fn count_chars(s: &String) -> usize`. A colleague suggests changing the parameter to `&str`. Explain the change and why it is an improvement.

required_points: Deref coercion: &String automatically coerces to &str
&str is more general — accepts string literals, slices, and owned Strings
The function does not need ownership or a mutable reference, so &str is the most flexible borrow type
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May phrase deref coercion differently; credit any mention of accepting literals.
answer_key.full_credit: Explains deref coercion and that &str is strictly more flexible with no performance cost.
answer_key.partial_credit: Says &str is 'better' or 'more idiomatic' without explaining why.

questions.prompt: A Java developer says: 'Garbage collection handles memory automatically and safely — why would I want ownership rules that force me to think about memory?' Write a response explaining the trade-offs: what problems GC solves that Rust also solves, and what advantages Rust's ownership model has over GC.
questions.guidance: Strong response covers: (1) both GC and ownership prevent use-after-free; (2) GC has runtime overhead and pause times — ownership is zero-cost; (3) ownership prevents data races at compile time, which GC does not; (4) GC doesn't help with lifetimes in systems code (e.g., interrupt handlers, embedded).

rubric.A: Addresses runtime overhead, data race prevention, and at least one systems programming context with specific examples.
rubric.B: Addresses overhead and data races but is vague about systems programming.
rubric.C: Only mentions performance without addressing safety guarantees.

questions.question: What happens if a `match` expression in Rust does not cover all variants of an enum?

options.A: The unmatched variants are silently ignored
options.B: The program panics at runtime when an unmatched variant is hit
options.C: The code does not compile — match must be exhaustive
options.D: The first arm acts as a default for unmatched variants

questions.explanation: Rust enforces exhaustive matching at compile time. If any variant is unhandled, the compiler reports an error listing the missing patterns. This is the key safety guarantee: forgotten cases cannot exist in shipped code.

questions.question:
Given:
```rust
enum Message {
    Quit,
    Move { x: i32, y: i32 },
    Write(String),
}
```
How do you access the `x` value from a `Message::Move` variant?

options.A: msg.x
options.B: Message::Move.x
options.C: if msg == Move { msg.x } else { 0 }
options.D: match msg { Message::Move { x, y } => x, _ => 0 }

questions.explanation: Enum variant data must be accessed through pattern matching. The struct-like variant `Move { x, y }` is destructured inside the match arm. Options A and B are not valid Rust syntax for enums.

questions.question: What does `Option::map(|v| v * 2)` return when called on `None`?

options.B: None
options.C: Some(0)
options.D: A compile error — map requires a Some value

questions.explanation: Option::map applies the closure only if the value is Some(v), returning Some(closure(v)). If the Option is None, map short-circuits and returns None without invoking the closure.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about Rust's `Option<T>` compared to null in Java or JavaScript?

options.A: Option<T> is slower because it wraps the value in an enum
options.B: The compiler forces you to handle the None case before accessing the inner value
options.C: Option<T> can hold multiple None values simultaneously
options.D: Option<T> is only available in the standard library, not user code

questions.explanation: The key advantage is compile-time enforcement. To access the inner T, you must handle the None case through match, if let, unwrap_or, map, etc. This eliminates null pointer exceptions entirely.

questions.question: When is `if let` preferred over `match`?

options.A: When you need to handle all variants of an enum
options.B: When you only care about one specific pattern and want to ignore the rest
options.C: When matching integers but not enums
options.D: When the enum has more than 5 variants

questions.explanation: `if let` is syntactic sugar for a match with exactly one handled arm and a `_ => ()` catch-all. Use it when you only need to act on one case — it's more concise than match. If you need all cases, use match.

questions.question: What does the `@` binding operator do in a match pattern?

options.A: It marks a match arm as unreachable
options.B: It tests the value against a range AND binds it to a variable simultaneously
options.C: It calls a function on the matched value
options.D: It is the syntax for matching string patterns

questions.explanation: The `@` operator lets you test a value against a pattern (e.g., a range) and bind the value to a name in the same arm: `n @ 1..=5 => println!("got {n}")`. Without @, you could test OR bind, but not both.

questions.question: Explain how Rust enums with data-carrying variants eliminate the need for certain class hierarchies in object-oriented languages. Give a concrete example.

required_points: Enum variants can hold different types and amounts of data
A single type captures all variants; no subclassing needed
match enforces handling all variants — unlike virtual dispatch where cases can be missed
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Any example of replacing an OOP hierarchy with a Rust enum is valid (e.g., Shape, Event, Message).
answer_key.full_credit: Concrete enum example with 2+ variants holding data, explanation of exhaustive match, contrast with inheritance.
answer_key.partial_credit: Describes the concept correctly but without a concrete example.

questions.question: What is `Option::and_then` and how does it differ from `Option::map`? Show a one-line example.

required_points: map applies a closure that returns T, wrapping the result in Some
and_then applies a closure that itself returns Option<T>, avoiding double-wrapping (Some(Some(x)))
and_then is used for chaining fallible operations
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Example: `Some(5).and_then(|v| if v > 3 { Some(v) } else { None })`
answer_key.full_credit: Correct contrast of return types (T vs Option<T>) with a valid example.
answer_key.partial_credit: Describes one correctly but confuses the other.

questions.prompt: Compare Rust's `match` expression with `switch` statements in C or Java. What categories of bugs does Rust's approach prevent? Use at least one concrete code example to illustrate.
questions.guidance: Strong responses cover: (1) exhaustiveness — Rust requires all arms; switch falls through or uses default; (2) structural pattern matching — Rust can destructure enums, structs, tuples; switch can only compare primitives; (3) no fallthrough — each arm is isolated; (4) concrete bug example such as an unhandled enum variant in Java/C causing a silent incorrect default.

rubric.A: Addresses exhaustiveness, structural matching, and no-fallthrough with concrete code examples for at least two points.
rubric.B: Correctly addresses exhaustiveness and one other point with an example.
rubric.C: Describes exhaustiveness only without code examples.

questions.question: What does `vec.get(5)` return when the Vec has only 3 elements?

options.A: 0 (the default value)
options.B: A panic — index out of bounds
options.C: None
options.D: A compile error

questions.explanation: .get(i) returns Option<&T> — None if the index is out of bounds. Direct indexing with vec[5] would panic at runtime. .get() is the safe choice when the index may be out of range.

options.A: Replaces Alice's score with 0
options.B: Inserts 0 for Alice only if Alice is not already in the map; returns a mutable reference to the value
options.C: Returns None if Alice is missing; returns Some(&value) if present
options.D: Inserts Alice unconditionally and panics if Alice already exists

questions.explanation: The entry API checks if the key exists. .or_insert(v) inserts v only when the key is absent and returns a &mut to the value in either case. This is the idiomatic Rust pattern for initialise-or-update.

questions.question: Why does Rust disallow `my_string[0]` for a String?

options.A: Strings are immutable in Rust
options.B: String is stored on the heap, so indexing requires an unsafe block
options.C: String is UTF-8 encoded; byte 0 may be the middle of a multi-byte character
options.D: There is no 0-based indexing in Rust; all collections use 1-based indexing

questions.explanation: Rust refuses to let you index a String by integer because the result would be ambiguous — is it a byte, a char, or a grapheme cluster? UTF-8 characters can span 1–4 bytes. Use .chars().nth(0) or .bytes().nth(0) depending on what you need.

questions.question: What is the difference between `.iter()` and `.into_iter()` on a Vec<T>?

options.A: .iter() is faster; .into_iter() is safer
options.B: .iter() yields &T (borrows the Vec); .into_iter() consumes the Vec and yields T
options.C: .iter() yields T; .into_iter() yields &T
options.D: They are identical — both borrow the Vec

questions.explanation: .iter() borrows the Vec and yields shared references (&T) — the Vec is still usable afterward. .into_iter() moves the Vec and yields owned values (T) — the Vec is consumed and no longer accessible.

questions.question: When you insert a String key into a HashMap, what happens to the key?

options.A: The HashMap stores a clone; the original is still valid
options.B: The HashMap stores a reference; you must keep the original alive
options.C: The key is moved into the HashMap; the original binding is no longer valid
options.D: Keys are always copied regardless of type

questions.explanation: HashMap takes ownership of keys and values that do not implement Copy. After inserting a String key, the original variable is moved and cannot be used unless you first clone it.

questions.question: Which method correctly counts the number of Unicode characters (not bytes) in a Rust String?

options.A: my_string.len()
options.B: my_string.count()
options.C: my_string.chars().count()
options.D: my_string.bytes().count()

questions.explanation: .len() returns the number of bytes, which may be larger than the character count for non-ASCII UTF-8 strings. .chars() produces an iterator over Unicode scalar values; .count() on that gives the character count.

questions.question: Describe the HashMap entry API pattern for counting word occurrences. Write the core loop (3–5 lines) using `.entry().or_insert(0)` and explain each step.

required_points: Calls .entry(word) to get an Entry for the key
Calls .or_insert(0) to initialise to 0 if missing, returning &mut u32
Dereferences the &mut and increments with += 1
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Variable names and exact string splitting method may vary.
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct logic but forgets to dereference the &mut (missing *count).

questions.question: When would you choose a Vec over a HashMap, and vice versa? Give one concrete use case for each.

required_points: Vec: ordered, indexed by position, O(1) push/index — use for sequences (e.g., task queue, sorted list)
HashMap: key-value lookup, O(1) average get/insert — use for associative data (e.g., user sessions by ID, word counts)
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Any valid and distinct use cases accepted.
answer_key.full_credit: Clear distinction with concrete use case per collection type.
answer_key.partial_credit: Identifies Vec or HashMap correctly but only one.

questions.prompt: You are building a text analytics tool that reads a document and reports: total word count, unique word count, and the top-5 most frequent words. Describe the data structures you would use (Vec, HashMap, or both), why you chose them, and sketch the core algorithm — including how the HashMap entry API handles repeated words.
questions.guidance: Strong response: (1) uses a HashMap<String, u32> for frequency counting; (2) explains why Vec<(String, u32)> is useful after counting (for sorting); (3) walks through the entry pattern for counting; (4) derives unique word count from HashMap's key count.

rubric.A: Correctly uses both HashMap and Vec, explains the entry API for counting, derives unique count from map keys, and sketches sort for top-5.
rubric.B: Correct collection choices and entry API, but doesn't explain how to get top-5 or unique count.
rubric.C: Identifies HashMap for counting but doesn't demonstrate entry API knowledge.

questions.question: What is monomorphization in Rust?

options.A: Generating a single runtime dispatch table for all generic types
options.B: The compiler generating a concrete function for each type used with a generic
options.C: A technique for converting trait objects to concrete types at runtime
options.D: The process of boxing generic values to send them across threads

questions.explanation: Monomorphization means the Rust compiler creates a separate, concrete copy of each generic function or struct for every type it is instantiated with. The result is zero runtime overhead — no boxing, no dispatch table.

questions.question: What does the orphan rule prevent?

options.A: Using the same trait in two different files
options.B: Implementing a trait from an external crate on a type from another external crate
options.C: Defining a trait and its implementation in the same module
options.D: Using default trait methods alongside custom implementations

questions.explanation: The orphan rule states: you can implement a trait for a type only if either the trait or the type is defined in your crate. This prevents two crates from providing conflicting implementations of the same trait for the same type.

questions.question: When must you write explicit lifetime annotations on a function?

options.A: Whenever the function takes more than one parameter
options.B: Only when the function returns a reference and the compiler cannot determine which input reference the output borrows from
options.C: Whenever the function takes a &str parameter
options.D: Only in unsafe functions

questions.explanation: Rust applies lifetime elision rules automatically in most cases. Explicit annotations are needed when the compiler cannot determine the relationship between input and output reference lifetimes — typically when there are multiple reference inputs and the output reference could come from any of them.

questions.question: What is the key difference between `impl Trait` parameter syntax and `dyn Trait` (a trait object)?

options.A: impl Trait uses runtime dispatch; dyn Trait uses compile-time dispatch
options.B: impl Trait resolves the concrete type at compile time (static dispatch); dyn Trait dispatches at runtime through a vtable
options.C: impl Trait and dyn Trait are identical — one is just newer syntax
options.D: dyn Trait is only for return types; impl Trait is only for parameters

questions.explanation: `impl Trait` (static dispatch) is monomorphized: the compiler generates concrete code per type, which is faster. `dyn Trait` stores a fat pointer (data pointer + vtable pointer) and dispatches method calls at runtime — slower but allows heterogeneous collections.

questions.question: What does the `'static` lifetime bound mean on a reference?

options.A: The referenced data is stored in static (global) memory and lives for the entire program
options.B: The reference cannot be passed to functions
options.C: The reference has been declared with the static keyword
options.D: The referenced type implements the Copy trait

questions.explanation: 'static means the reference is valid for the entire duration of the program. String literals have 'static lifetime because they are embedded in the binary. A common mistake is adding 'static to silence borrow errors — this is almost always wrong.

questions.question: You want a function that accepts any type implementing both `Debug` and `Clone`. Which signature is correct?

options.A: fn process(x: T) where Debug + Clone
options.B: fn process<T: Debug + Clone>(x: T)
options.C: fn process(x: impl Debug | Clone)
options.D: fn process(x: dyn Debug + dyn Clone)

questions.explanation: Multiple trait bounds are written with `+`: `T: Debug + Clone`. The where clause equivalent is `where T: Debug + Clone`. Option C uses | which is not valid for trait bounds. Option D creates two separate trait objects, which is invalid syntax.

questions.question: State Rust's three lifetime elision rules. For each rule, give a one-sentence example of when it applies.

required_points: Rule 1: Each reference parameter gets its own lifetime — fn foo(x: &i32) becomes fn foo<'a>(x: &'a i32)
Rule 2: If there is exactly one input lifetime, it is assigned to all output lifetimes — fn first(s: &str) -> &str
Rule 3: If one input is &self or &mut self, its lifetime is assigned to all outputs — method with self returning a reference
answer_key.full_credit: All three rules with correct examples.
answer_key.partial_credit: Two of three rules correctly stated.

questions.question: Define a trait `Summary` with a required method `summarize(&self) -> String` and a default method `preview(&self) -> String` that returns the first 100 characters of summarize(). Write the trait definition only (not the implementation).

required_points: Correct trait keyword and name
summarize declared without a body (required method)
preview implemented with a default body calling self.summarize()
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Exact character count cutoff may vary; &self[..100] or chars().take(100) both acceptable.
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct structure but missing default implementation or incorrect syntax.

questions.prompt: Explain how generics, traits, and lifetimes work together in Rust to achieve code reuse without runtime cost. Use a concrete example — such as a generic sorting function with a trait bound — to illustrate each concept's role.
questions.guidance: Strong response: (1) generics = write once, compile to concrete types (no boxing); (2) trait bounds = constrain what operations T supports (compile-time checked); (3) lifetimes = guarantee references are valid as long as the algorithm runs; (4) concrete example tying all three together.

rubric.A: Covers all three concepts with correct roles, concrete example tying them together, and mentions zero-cost.
rubric.B: Covers generics and traits correctly with an example; lifetime explanation vague.
rubric.C: Describes generics only; traits and lifetimes are absent or incorrect.

questions.question: What makes a closure `FnOnce` instead of `Fn`?

options.A: The closure captures variables by mutable reference
options.B: The closure moves a captured value out of its body, so it can only be called once
options.C: The closure takes exactly one argument
options.D: The closure is defined with the move keyword

questions.explanation: FnOnce closures consume a captured variable by moving it out. Since the value is gone after the first call, the closure can only be called once. Fn can be called any number of times; FnMut requires a mutable capture.

questions.question: Why are iterator adapters (map, filter, etc.) described as 'lazy' in Rust?

options.A: They execute asynchronously in the background
options.B: They do nothing until a consuming adaptor (collect, sum, etc.) drives the iterator
options.C: They are slower than explicit loops because they defer work
options.D: They cache results to avoid recomputation

questions.explanation: Iterator adapters are lazy: they return a new iterator struct but perform no computation until a consuming adaptor calls next(). This avoids allocating intermediate collections and lets the compiler optimize the entire chain into a single loop.

questions.question: What does `.enumerate()` add to an iterator?

options.A: Converts the iterator to a Vec
options.B: Pairs each item with its zero-based index as a (usize, T) tuple
options.C: Counts the total number of items and returns a usize
options.D: Groups items into chunks of fixed size
