questions.explanation: .enumerate() wraps each element in a (index, value) tuple, where index is a usize starting at 0. This is the idiomatic way to iterate with position: `for (i, val) in v.iter().enumerate() { ... }`

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes the difference between `iter()` and `into_iter()` on a Vec<T>?

options.A: iter() moves the Vec; into_iter() borrows it
options.B: iter() yields &T (borrows elements); into_iter() consumes the Vec and yields T
options.C: They are identical — into_iter() is just the older name
options.D: into_iter() is only available on HashMap, not Vec

questions.explanation: .iter() borrows the Vec and yields shared references &T. .into_iter() consumes the Vec (takes ownership) and yields owned values T. After .into_iter(), the original Vec can no longer be used.

questions.question: What does `.collect::<Vec<_>>()` do at the end of an iterator chain?

options.A: Frees all items in the iterator
options.B: Counts the number of items and returns a usize
options.C: Drives the iterator to completion and assembles results into a Vec
options.D: Returns an iterator that replays the chain from the beginning

questions.explanation: .collect() is a consuming adaptor that drives the entire iterator chain, calling next() until exhausted, and assembles the results into the specified collection type. Without a consuming adaptor, the chain does nothing.

questions.question: You have a Vec<Option<i32>>. Which iterator adapter produces a Vec<i32> containing only the Some values, unwrapped?

options.A: .map(|x| x.unwrap())
options.B: .filter(|x| x.is_some()).map(|x| x.unwrap())
options.C: .flat_map(|x| x)
options.D: .filter_map(|x| x)

questions.explanation: .filter_map(|x| x) calls the closure on each element; if it returns Some(v), v is included; if None, the element is skipped. Options A panics on None; option B works but is verbose; option C (flat_map) works here but filter_map is more idiomatic.

required_points: Uses .iter() or .into_iter() on numbers
Chains .filter() for the even check
Chains .map() for the *3 transformation
Calls .collect() to produce the Vec
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Can use filter_map; can use copied() or & deref patterns.
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct structure but incorrect deref of references (&& pattern).

questions.question: Explain the difference between `Fn`, `FnMut`, and `FnOnce`. Why does Rust have three traits instead of one?

required_points: Fn: captures by shared reference, callable any number of times
FnMut: captures by mutable reference, callable multiple times but requires exclusive access
FnOnce: moves captured values, callable exactly once
Three traits allow functions to express the minimum capability they need
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May describe in terms of what the closure does to captured values.
answer_key.full_credit: All three defined correctly with the rationale that using the least restrictive bound makes APIs more flexible.

questions.prompt: Rust claims iterator chains are zero-cost abstractions — they compile to code as efficient as hand-written loops. Explain what 'zero-cost abstraction' means, how it applies to iterator adapters, and why this makes functional-style code preferable to index loops in Rust.
questions.guidance: Strong response: (1) zero-cost = no runtime overhead compared to the manually written equivalent; (2) iterator chains are inlined and fused by the optimizer — no intermediate allocations; (3) functional chains are safer (no off-by-one, no manual indexing) and often more readable; (4) concrete comparison of an index loop vs its iterator-chain equivalent.

rubric.A: Defines zero-cost correctly, explains inlining/fusion, gives a concrete example comparing loop vs chain, notes safety benefits.
rubric.B: Correct definition and iterator example but doesn't explain the optimizer or intermediate allocations.
rubric.C: Describes lazy evaluation but doesn't connect to zero-cost or inlining.

questions.question: Why must closures passed to `thread::spawn` typically use the `move` keyword?

options.A: move is required syntax for all thread closures — it is not optional
options.B: The spawned thread may outlive the current scope; move transfers ownership so the thread doesn't hold dangling references
options.C: move prevents the compiler from optimising the closure
options.D: Without move, the thread cannot access variables from the outer scope

questions.explanation: The thread::spawn call gives no lifetime guarantee — the spawned thread may run past the end of the enclosing function. Without move, captured references could dangle. Moving ownership into the thread eliminates this risk.

questions.question: What does `JoinHandle::join()` return?

options.A: The thread ID of the finished thread
options.B: () — it only synchronises, not returns a value
options.C: Result<T, Box<dyn Any + Send>> — the thread's return value or panic payload
options.D: Option<T> — Some(value) if the thread completed normally, None if it panicked

questions.explanation: .join() blocks until the thread finishes and returns a Result: Ok(T) where T is the closure's return value, or Err if the thread panicked (the panic payload is a Box<dyn Any>).

questions.question: What does mpsc stand for and what constraint does it describe?

options.A: Multiple Protocol Synchronised Communication — allows any number of producers and consumers
options.B: Multiple Producer, Single Consumer — many senders, one receiver
options.C: Message Passing Synchronised Channel — describes the blocking behaviour of recv
options.D: Mutex Protected Shared Context — wraps a Mutex around the channel

questions.explanation: mpsc = Multiple Producer, Single Consumer. The Sender<T> can be cloned to allow multiple producers, but there is only one Receiver<T>. If you need multiple consumers, you need a different channel type (e.g., from the crossbeam crate).

questions.question: Why can't `Rc<T>` be used to share data across threads?

options.A: Rc<T> is too slow for multi-threaded use
options.B: Rc<T> does not implement the Send trait because its reference counting is not atomic
options.C: Rc<T> is deprecated in favour of Arc<T> even in single-threaded code
options.D: The compiler allows it but panics at runtime if two threads access the counter simultaneously

questions.explanation: Rc<T> uses non-atomic (non-thread-safe) reference counting, so it doesn't implement Send. The compiler rejects sending Rc<T> to another thread at compile time. Arc<T> uses atomic operations to make reference counting thread-safe.

questions.question: What happens when a `MutexGuard<T>` is dropped?

options.A: The Mutex itself is destroyed
options.B: The lock is automatically released via the Drop trait
options.C: The inner value T is deallocated
options.D: Other threads waiting on the lock receive an error

questions.explanation: MutexGuard implements Drop — when it goes out of scope, the lock is automatically released. This is RAII: you cannot forget to unlock because the unlock is tied to the guard's lifetime, not a manual call.

questions.question: You have a function that takes `Arc<Mutex<Vec<String>>>`. To add an item, what is the correct sequence?

options.A: arc.push(item) — Arc provides direct access
options.B: arc.lock().unwrap().push(item) — lock the Mutex to get the guard, call push on the guard
options.C: arc.borrow_mut().push(item) — Arc implements RefCell methods
options.D: unsafe { arc.get_mut().push(item) } — Arc requires unsafe for mutation

questions.explanation: Arc<Mutex<T>> requires calling .lock() to acquire the Mutex guard, which returns a MutexGuard<T> that derefs to T. Then .push() is called on the guard. The lock is released when the guard drops at end of statement.

questions.question: Explain how Rust prevents data races at compile time. Name the two marker traits involved and what each guarantees.

required_points: Send: a type can be moved safely to another thread
Sync: a type can be shared (referenced) across threads safely (T is Sync if &T is Send)
The compiler checks Send/Sync at spawn sites; types that don't implement these cannot cross thread boundaries
Combined with ownership (no aliased mutable access), this eliminates data races statically
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May phrase Sync differently; key is that both traits are named and defined.
answer_key.full_credit: Both Send and Sync defined correctly with explanation of how ownership + these traits prevent data races.
answer_key.partial_credit: Names one trait correctly or describes the mechanism without naming the traits.

questions.question: When would you choose message passing (mpsc channels) over shared state (Arc<Mutex<T>>) for inter-thread communication? Give one use case for each.

required_points: Channels: prefer when threads have a producer/consumer relationship and data flows one-way (e.g., worker pool feeding results to aggregator)
Arc<Mutex>: prefer when multiple threads need to read/write the same shared structure (e.g., a shared cache or counter incremented by many threads)
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Any valid and distinct use case for each.
answer_key.full_credit: Clear distinction with a concrete, plausible use case for each.

questions.prompt: Rust claims 'fearless concurrency.' Explain how the ownership system — combined with the Send and Sync marker traits, Arc<T>, and Mutex<T> — makes this claim defensible. Compare to how data races are handled in Python (GIL) or Java (synchronized keyword).
questions.guidance: Strong response: (1) ownership prevents aliased mutation → the root cause of data races; (2) Send/Sync enforce thread-safety at compile time — not at runtime; (3) Arc + Mutex provide safe shared state; (4) Python GIL is a runtime lock preventing all multi-core parallelism; Java synchronized is runtime, can be forgotten; (5) Rust: the compiler refuses to compile unsafe patterns.

rubric.A: Explains ownership + Send/Sync mechanism, Arc/Mutex pattern, and meaningfully contrasts with Python or Java with specific examples.
rubric.B: Covers Rust's mechanism and one comparison language correctly, but comparison lacks specifics.
rubric.C: Describes Arc/Mutex correctly but doesn't explain Send/Sync or the ownership connection.

questions.q: What command creates a new Rust project, and what two files does it generate inside the project directory?
questions.a: `cargo new <name>` creates `src/main.rs` (the entry point with a hello-world stub) and `Cargo.toml` (the project manifest that lists metadata and dependencies).

questions.q: What is variable shadowing in Rust, and how does it differ from declaring a variable with `mut`?
questions.a: Shadowing re-declares a name with a new `let` binding, allowing a type change and creating a fresh immutable binding. `mut` allows in-place value mutation but cannot change the type.

questions.q: What is the difference between an expression and a statement in Rust? Give one example of each.
questions.a: An expression evaluates to a value (e.g., `5 + 3` evaluates to `8`). A statement performs an action and returns `()` — e.g., `let x = 5;` is a statement and cannot appear on the right side of another `let`.

questions.q:
Why does this Rust function return a value without the `return` keyword?

```rust
fn double(n: i32) -> i32 { n * 2 }
```
questions.a: Rust functions return the value of the last expression in their body when there is no trailing semicolon. Adding a semicolon turns it into a statement returning `()`, which would be a type error here.

questions.q: State Rust's three ownership rules.
questions.a: (1) Each value in Rust has exactly one owner. (2) There can only be one owner at a time. (3) When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped (Drop is called).

questions.q: What is the difference between a move and a copy in Rust? Name one type that is copied and one that is moved on assignment.
questions.a: A copy duplicates the value on the stack; both bindings remain valid. A move transfers ownership; the original is no longer accessible. `i32` is copied; `String` is moved.

questions.q: State Rust's two borrow rules and explain how they prevent data races.
questions.a: Rule 1: You can have any number of immutable references (&T) at a time. Rule 2: You can have at most one mutable reference (&mut T), and no immutable references at the same time. Together, these ensure no two threads can read-and-write the same location concurrently.

questions.q: When should a function parameter be `&str` instead of `&String`, and why?
questions.a: `&str` is preferred because deref coercion allows it to accept both string literals (`&'static str`) and references to owned Strings (`&String` → `&str` automatically). Using `&String` unnecessarily restricts callers to only owned Strings.

questions.q: What is the difference between a method with `&self`, `&mut self`, and `self` as the first parameter?
questions.a: `&self` borrows the struct immutably — the method can read but not modify. `&mut self` borrows mutably — the method can modify fields. `self` takes ownership — the struct is consumed and cannot be used after the call.

questions.q: What is an associated function in Rust, and how is it different from a method? Give an example.
questions.a: An associated function has no `self` parameter — it is called on the type with `::` syntax rather than on an instance. Example: `String::from("hello")` or a custom `Point::new(x, y)` constructor.

questions.q: What does `#[derive(Debug)]` enable on a struct, and how do you use it in a println?
questions.a: `#[derive(Debug)]` automatically implements the `Debug` trait, which provides a developer-friendly string representation. Use it with the `{:?}` or `{:#?}` (pretty-print) format specifiers: `println!("{:?}", my_struct);`

questions.q: Why does Rust recommend using owned types like `String` (not `&str`) inside structs until you have learned lifetimes?
questions.a: Struct fields that are references require lifetime annotations to tell the compiler how long the referenced data must live. Owned types like `String` don't need annotations because the struct owns the data — it's simpler until lifetimes are understood.

M04.title: Enums, Pattern Matching & Option — Quick Check

questions.q: Why must a `match` expression in Rust cover all variants of an enum?
questions.a: Rust enforces exhaustive matching at compile time. If any variant is unhandled the code will not compile, making it impossible to ship code that silently ignores a case. This eliminates a whole class of runtime bugs common in switch-based languages.

questions.q: What is `Option<T>` and what problem does it solve compared to null in other languages?
questions.a: `Option<T>` is an enum with two variants: `Some(T)` when a value is present, `None` when absent. Unlike null, the compiler forces you to handle both variants before accessing the inner value — eliminating null pointer exceptions entirely.

questions.q: What is the difference between `if let` and `match`? When would you prefer each?
questions.a: `if let` is syntactic sugar for a match with one handled arm and an implicit `_ => ()` fallthrough. Use `if let` when you only care about one pattern; use `match` when you need to handle multiple variants, especially for exhaustiveness.

questions.q: How do you extract data from an enum variant that carries a value? Write a one-line example.
questions.a: Use pattern matching to destructure: `if let Some(value) = my_option { ... }` or inside a match arm: `Message::Write(text) => println!("{text}")`. You cannot access variant data directly like a struct field.

questions.q: What does `vec.get(i)` return that `vec[i]` does not, and why is this the safer choice for potentially-out-of-bounds access?
questions.a: `.get(i)` returns `Option<&T>` — `None` if the index is out of bounds. Direct indexing with `vec[i]` panics at runtime if `i` is out of range. `.get()` forces the caller to handle the missing case explicitly.

questions.q: Explain the HashMap entry API: what does `.entry(k).or_insert(v)` do when the key is present vs absent?
questions.a: When the key is absent, it inserts the provided value `v` and returns a `&mut` to it. When the key is present, it skips the insertion and returns a `&mut` to the existing value. Either way the return is a mutable reference, which lets you increment in place.

questions.q: Why does Rust disallow `my_string[0]` for a String, unlike arrays or Vec?
questions.a: String is UTF-8 encoded, and characters can span 1–4 bytes. Indexing by integer could land in the middle of a multi-byte character, producing invalid data. Rust refuses this at compile time; use `.chars().nth(i)` for characters or `.bytes().nth(i)` for raw bytes.

questions.q: What is the difference between `.iter()` and `.into_iter()` on a Vec?
questions.a: `.iter()` borrows the Vec and yields `&T` references — the Vec remains usable after. `.into_iter()` consumes the Vec and yields owned `T` values — the Vec is no longer accessible after the call.

questions.q: What is `Result<T, E>` and when should you use it instead of `panic!`?
questions.a: `Result<T, E>` is an enum with `Ok(T)` for success and `Err(E)` for failure. Use `Result` for recoverable errors (file not found, parse failure, network timeout) where the caller can meaningfully handle the problem. Use `panic!` only for unrecoverable programming errors (broken invariants, logic bugs).

questions.q: What does the `?` operator do, and what return types can it be used in?
questions.a: `?` is syntactic sugar for: if the value is `Ok(v)`, unwrap to `v`; if `Err(e)`, call `From::from(e)` to convert the error type and return early with that error. It can only be used in functions returning `Result<_, E>` or `Option<_>` (and `main()` if it returns `Result`).

questions.q: When would you write a custom error type instead of using `Box<dyn Error>`?
questions.a: Custom error types are preferred in library code where callers need to match on specific error variants, when you want structured error data (e.g., line number, field name), or when you need to document the exact failure modes in the public API. `Box<dyn Error>` is fine for applications and prototypes.

questions.q: What must a custom error type implement to work with the `?` operator and standard error handling?
questions.a: It must implement `std::fmt::Display` (for human-readable messages) and `std::error::Error` (the error trait). To use `?` for automatic type conversion, you also implement `From<OtherError> for MyError`, which `?` calls via `From::from`.

questions.q: What is monomorphization and why does it make generic Rust code zero-cost?
questions.a: Monomorphization is the compiler generating a concrete copy of each generic function or struct for every type it is instantiated with. There is no boxing or runtime dispatch — the resulting machine code is identical to what you would write by hand for each specific type.

questions.q: What does the orphan rule say, and why does it exist?
questions.a: The orphan rule says: you can implement a trait for a type only if the trait or the type (or both) are defined in your crate. It prevents two crates from providing conflicting implementations of the same trait for the same type, which would be ambiguous.

questions.q: When do you need to write lifetime annotations explicitly, and when can you omit them?
questions.a: You can omit lifetimes (elision) when: (1) each reference parameter gets an independent lifetime, (2) there is exactly one input reference (it applies to output), or (3) one input is `&self`/`&mut self` (its lifetime applies to output). You must write annotations when there are multiple input references and the compiler cannot determine which one the output borrows from.

questions.q: What is the difference between `impl Trait` (static dispatch) and `dyn Trait` (dynamic dispatch)? When would you choose each?
questions.a: `impl Trait` is resolved at compile time — the compiler generates concrete code per type (fast, zero-cost, but the type must be known at compile time). `dyn Trait` dispatches through a vtable at runtime (small overhead, but allows storing heterogeneous types in a collection such as `Vec<Box<dyn Trait>>`).

questions.q: What is the difference between `Fn`, `FnMut`, and `FnOnce`?
questions.a: `Fn`: captures by immutable reference; can be called any number of times. `FnMut`: captures by mutable reference; can be called multiple times but requires exclusive access. `FnOnce`: moves captured values out of the closure body; can only be called once because the captured value is consumed.

questions.q: What does 'lazy evaluation' mean in the context of Rust iterators? Why does it matter?
questions.a: Iterator adapters (map, filter, etc.) build a chain of structs but perform no computation until a consuming adaptor (sum, collect, for-loop) drives them by calling `next()`. This means no intermediate collections are allocated and the compiler can fuse the whole chain into a single optimised loop.

questions.q: What is the difference between `.iter()` and `.into_iter()` on a `Vec<T>`?
questions.a: `.iter()` borrows the Vec and yields `&T` references — the Vec is still usable after. `.into_iter()` consumes the Vec, taking ownership, and yields `T` values directly — the Vec cannot be used afterward.

questions.q: Describe `.filter_map()` in one sentence and give a use case where it is more concise than `.filter().map()`.
questions.a: `.filter_map(f)` applies a closure that returns `Option<B>` and keeps only `Some(b)` values, discarding `None` — combining filter and unwrap in one step. Use case: extracting and parsing only the lines in a text file that are valid integers, skipping blank lines and errors.

questions.q: Why do closures passed to `thread::spawn` usually need the `move` keyword?
questions.a: The spawned thread may outlive the scope where the closure was created. Without `move`, the closure could hold references to local variables that get dropped — a dangling reference. `move` transfers ownership of captured values into the thread, ensuring they live as long as the thread does.

questions.q: What does `Arc<Mutex<T>>` provide that a plain `Mutex<T>` cannot?
questions.a: `Mutex<T>` alone cannot be shared across threads because it doesn't implement `Clone`. `Arc<T>` provides reference-counted, thread-safe shared ownership via atomic operations (`Arc` implements `Send` and `Sync`). Together, `Arc<Mutex<T>>` allows multiple threads to safely share and mutate the same value.

questions.q: When would you prefer channels (`mpsc`) over shared state (`Arc<Mutex<T>>`) for inter-thread communication?
questions.a: Prefer channels when threads have a clear producer/consumer relationship and data flows one-way — e.g., worker threads producing results for a single aggregator. Prefer `Arc<Mutex<T>>` when multiple threads need concurrent read/write access to the same data structure, such as a shared cache or counter.

questions.q: What prevents data races at compile time in Rust? Name the two marker traits and what each guarantees.
questions.a: `Send`: the type can be safely transferred (moved) to another thread. `Sync`: the type can be safely shared by reference (`&T`) across threads. The compiler checks these at `thread::spawn` call sites. Combined with ownership (no aliased mutable access), these make data races a compile-time error.

challenges.prompt: Write a function `celsius_to_fahrenheit(c: f64) -> f64` that converts a Celsius temperature to Fahrenheit. The formula is `F = C × 1.8 + 32`. Round to two decimal places by using `(value * 100.0).round() / 100.0`.

Write a function `fizzbuzz(n: u32) -> String` that returns `"FizzBuzz"` if n is divisible by both 3 and 5, `"Fizz"` if divisible by 3 only, `"Buzz"` if divisible by 5 only, or the number as a string otherwise. Use `if`/`else if`/`else` — **not** `match`.
Write a function `sum_range(n: u32) -> u32` that returns the sum of all integers from 1 to `n` inclusive (i.e. 1 + 2 + … + n). Use a `for` loop with a range.

Write a function `first_word(s: &str) -> &str` that returns the first word (the part before the first space) from the input string slice. If there is no space, return the whole string. Do NOT clone or allocate — work with slices only.
Write a function `count_vowels(s: &str) -> usize` that counts the number of ASCII vowels (`a e i o u`, case-insensitive) in the given string slice. Iterate over `.chars()`.
Write a function `append_greeting(s: &mut String) -> usize` that appends `", world!"` to the given `String` (in place), then returns the new length of the string.

Implement a `Rectangle` struct with `width: f64` and `height: f64` fields. Add an `impl` block with:
- `new(width: f64, height: f64) -> Rectangle` — constructor
- `area(&self) -> f64` — returns width × height
- `is_square(&self) -> bool` — returns true if width == height

Write a function `describe_rectangle(width: f64, height: f64) -> String` that returns a string like `"area=12, square=false"` (area as integer if whole number, otherwise one decimal).

Implement a `Student` struct with fields `name: String` and `grades: Vec<f64>`. Add:
- `new(name: &str) -> Student`
- `add_grade(&mut self, grade: f64)` — appends to grades
- `average(&self) -> f64` — mean of grades, or 0.0 if empty

Write a function `student_average(name: &str, grades_csv: &str) -> f64` that parses a comma-separated list of grades (e.g. `"85.0,90.5,78.0"`), builds a Student, adds each grade, and returns the average rounded to 1 decimal place (`(avg * 10.0).round() / 10.0`).

M04.title: Coding Challenges: Enums, Pattern Matching & Option

challenges.prompt: Define an enum `Direction` with variants `North`, `South`, `East`, `West`. Write a function `opposite(d: Direction) -> Direction` that returns the opposite direction, and a function `direction_name(d: Direction) -> String` that returns the lowercase name string ("north", "south", "east", "west"). Write an entry function `direction_info(name: &str) -> String` that parses name into a Direction (matching "north"/"south"/"east"/"west"), then returns `"opposite of north is south"` format.

Write a function `safe_divide(a: f64, b: f64) -> Option<f64>` that returns `None` if `b` is zero and `Some(a / b)` otherwise. Then write `divide_or_default(a: f64, b: f64, default: f64) -> f64` that calls `safe_divide` and uses `.unwrap_or` to return the default on `None`. Round the result to 2 decimal places (`(v * 100.0).round() / 100.0`). Write an entry function `compute(input: &str) -> String` that parses `"a b default"` (three space-separated f64s) and returns the result as a string.

Write a function `classify_number(n: i64) -> String` that uses `match` to return:
- `"negative"` for any n < 0
- `"zero"` for n == 0
- `"small"` for 1..=9
- `"teen"` for 10..=19
- `"large"` for anything else

Use match with range patterns (`n if n < 0`, `1..=9`, etc.).

Write a function `vec_stats(numbers: Vec<f64>) -> String` that computes the minimum, maximum, and mean of the vector, then returns them as `"min=N max=N mean=N"`. All three values should be rounded to 1 decimal place using `(v * 10.0).round() / 10.0`. Write an entry function `stats(input: &str) -> String` that parses a space-separated list of floats and calls `vec_stats`.

Write a function `word_count(text: &str) -> String` that counts the frequency of each word in the input (split on whitespace, case-insensitive), then returns the counts as a sorted comma-separated string in the format `"word:count,word:count"` sorted alphabetically by word. Use a `HashMap`. Write an entry function `count_words(input: &str) -> String`.

Write a function `parse_positive(s: &str) -> Result<u32, String>` that:
1. Trims whitespace and parses `s` as a `u32`. If parsing fails, return `Err("not a number".to_string())`.
2. If the parsed number is 0, return `Err("must be positive".to_string())`.
3. Otherwise return `Ok(value)`.

Write an entry function `try_parse(input: &str) -> String` that calls `parse_positive` and returns either `"ok:N"` on success or `"err:message"` on failure.

Write a function `parse_and_sum(input: &str) -> Result<i64, String>` that takes a comma-separated string of integers (e.g. `"1,2,3"`), parses each, and returns their sum as `Ok(sum)`. If any token fails to parse as `i64`, return `Err("parse error: TOKEN")` where TOKEN is the offending token. Use the `?` operator where possible inside a helper.

Write an entry function `sum_csv(input: &str) -> String` that returns `"ok:N"` or `"err:parse error: TOKEN"`.

Write a generic function `largest<T: PartialOrd>(list: &[T]) -> &T` that returns a reference to the largest element in the slice. Then write an entry function `largest_int(input: &str) -> i64` that parses a space-separated list of integers, collects them into a `Vec<i64>`, and returns the largest.

Define a trait `Summary` with a method `summarize(&self) -> String`. Implement it for a struct `Article { title: String, author: String }` to return `"{title} by {author}"`. Write an entry function `summarize_article(title: &str, author: &str) -> String` that constructs an Article and calls `.summarize()`.

Write a function `longest_string<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str` that returns the longer of the two string slices (if equal length, return `x`). Write an entry function `compare(input: &str) -> String` that parses `"word1 word2"` (split on the first space) and returns the longer word.

Write a function `apply_twice<F: Fn(i64) -> i64>(f: F, x: i64) -> i64` that applies the closure `f` to `x` twice (i.e. `f(f(x))`). Write an entry function `double_twice(input: &str) -> i64` that parses `input` as an `i64` and calls `apply_twice` with a doubling closure (`|x| x * 2`).

Write a function `even_squares(n: u32) -> Vec<u32>` that returns the squares of even numbers in the range 1..=n using iterator adapters (no `for` loops). Then write an entry function `even_squares_sum(input: &str) -> u64` that parses `input` as a `u32` and returns the sum of the even squares.

Write a function `top_n_words(text: &str, n: usize) -> Vec<String>` that:
1. Splits `text` on whitespace
2. Counts word frequencies (lowercase) using a HashMap
3. Returns the top `n` words sorted by frequency descending, then alphabetically on ties

Write an entry function `top_words(input: &str) -> String` that parses `"N words..."` (first token is the integer n, rest is text), calls `top_n_words`, and joins the result with commas.

challenges.prompt: Write a function `parallel_sum(numbers: Vec<i64>) -> i64` that splits the input vector into two halves, spawns a thread for each half, computes the partial sum in each thread using a `move` closure, joins both handles, and returns the total sum. Write an entry function `sum_parallel(input: &str) -> i64` that parses a space-separated list of integers.

Write a function `shared_counter(increments: u32) -> u32` that uses `Arc<Mutex<u32>>` to share a counter across `increments` threads (spawn one thread per increment, each adding 1 to the shared counter), joins all threads, and returns the final counter value. Write an entry function `count_concurrent(input: &str) -> u32` that parses `input` as a `u32`.

applications.title: CLI Temperature Converter
applications.summary: Put Rust's functions, control flow, and Cargo to work by shipping a real command-line tool that converts temperatures between three scales.
applications.prompt: Build a Cargo project called `tempconv` that reads a temperature value and scale flag from the command line (`cargo run -- 100 C`) and prints the equivalent in the other two scales. Use `match` to dispatch on scale, `fn` for each conversion, and clean error messages for invalid input.
applications.deliverable: A runnable Cargo binary that handles F, C, and K inputs with correct output and a friendly error message for unknown scales.
steps: Run `cargo new tempconv --bin` and add a `main` that reads `std::env::args`.
Write one conversion function per pair (c_to_f, f_to_c, c_to_k, etc.) with explicit `-> f64` return types.
Use `match` on the scale argument to dispatch to the right pair of functions.
Print all three scales in a formatted table and test with edge values (−273.15 °C, 0 °F, 373.15 K).
Add a usage message when the argument count is wrong.
success_criteria: `cargo run -- 100 C` prints correct Fahrenheit and Kelvin values.
All conversion functions use explicit parameter and return types — no type inference on signatures.
Invalid scale inputs print a helpful error and exit with a non-zero code.
The project builds with `cargo build --release` without warnings.
extension_ideas: Add a `--round N` flag to control decimal precision.
Read a list of temperatures from a file and convert all of them.

applications.title: String Reversal Without Allocation
applications.summary: Prove you understand slices and borrowing by writing a sentence-reversal library that never clones the input string.
applications.prompt: Create a Cargo library crate `word_reverse` with a public function `reverse_words(s: &str) -> String` that reverses the order of words in a sentence (words separated by spaces) without allocating a copy of the input. The returned `String` is freshly constructed from slices only.
applications.deliverable: A library crate with at least six unit tests covering normal input, single words, multiple spaces, and empty strings.
steps: Run `cargo new word_reverse --lib`.
Write `reverse_words` to split the input into `&str` slices using `.split_whitespace()`.
Collect slices into a `Vec<&str>`, reverse the vec, and join with a single space — no intermediate `String` clones.
Add tests: `"hello world"` → `"world hello"`, `"rust"` → `"rust"`, `"  a  b  "` → `"b a"`, `""` → `""`.
Run `cargo clippy` and resolve any lint warnings.
success_criteria: The function signature is `fn reverse_words(s: &str) -> String` — no owned `String` input.
All unit tests pass with `cargo test`.
No `.clone()` call appears inside `reverse_words`.
Clippy is clean.
extension_ideas: Add a `reverse_chars(s: &str) -> String` that reverses individual characters, respecting multi-byte UTF-8.
Benchmark with `cargo bench` and compare with a naive clone-based version.

applications.title: Point and Shape Library
applications.summary: Apply structs, impl blocks, and derived traits to build a small 2D geometry library you'd actually import.
applications.prompt: Create a Cargo library `geometry` with three structs: `Point { x: f64, y: f64 }`, `Circle { center: Point, radius: f64 }`, and `Rectangle { top_left: Point, bottom_right: Point }`. Implement `area()` and `perimeter()` methods on both shapes, a `distance_from_origin(&self) -> f64` method on `Point`, and `Display` formatting that renders them like `Circle(center=(1.0, 2.0), r=3.0)`.
applications.deliverable: A library crate with `#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq)]` on all structs, `Display` implementations, and a suite of unit tests.
steps: Define the three structs with `#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq)]`.
Implement `Point::new(x, y)`, `Circle::new(center, radius)`, `Rectangle::new(top_left, bottom_right)` associated functions.
Add `area()` and `perimeter()` methods using `&self`.
Implement `std::fmt::Display` for all three types.
Write at least eight unit tests covering area, perimeter, display output, and clone equality.
success_criteria: All methods take `&self` — none mutate the struct.
Display output matches the specified format exactly in tests.
Clone and PartialEq work correctly in test assertions.
`cargo test` passes without warnings.
extension_ideas: Add a `contains(point: &Point) -> bool` method on both shapes.
Implement `From<(f64, f64)>` for `Point` so you can write `Point::from((1.0, 2.0))`.

M04.title: Practical applications — Enums, Pattern Matching & Option

applications.title: Mini Expression Evaluator
applications.summary: Use enums with data-carrying variants and exhaustive match to build a tiny expression tree that eliminates null-pointer bugs by design.
applications.prompt: Build a `calculator` library that represents arithmetic expressions as an enum `Expr { Num(f64), Add(Box<Expr>, Box<Expr>), Sub(Box<Expr>, Box<Expr>), Mul(Box<Expr>, Box<Expr>), Div(Box<Expr>, Box<Expr>) }` and evaluates them with `fn eval(expr: &Expr) -> Option<f64>` (returning `None` on division by zero).
applications.deliverable: A library crate where `eval` is fully exhaustive, all division-by-zero cases return `None`, and at least eight tests exercise different expression trees.
steps: Define the `Expr` enum with `Box<Expr>` for recursive variants.
Write `eval` using a recursive `match` — every variant must be handled.
Return `None` from the `Div` arm when the right operand evaluates to 0.
Use `Option::and_then` to propagate `None` through nested expressions.
Test trees that are two and three levels deep; test division by zero in a nested position.
success_criteria: The `match` in `eval` is exhaustive — removing a variant causes a compile error.
Division by zero at any depth returns `None`.
No `unwrap()` calls inside `eval` — only `?` or `and_then`.
All tests pass.
extension_ideas: Write a simple recursive-descent parser that converts `"3 + 4 * 2"` into an `Expr` tree.

applications.title: Word Frequency Counter
applications.summary: Combine Vec, HashMap, and String operations to build a production-quality text analysis tool.
applications.prompt: Build a Cargo binary `wordfreq` that reads text from stdin (or a file argument), counts word frequencies (case-insensitive, stripped of punctuation), and prints the top N most frequent words in descending order. N defaults to 10 and can be overridden with `--top N`.
applications.deliverable: A runnable binary that correctly handles multi-paragraph input and prints a ranked frequency table with counts.
steps: Read all of stdin into a `String` using `std::io::Read`.
Normalize: `to_lowercase()`, strip non-alphabetic chars using `.chars().filter(|c| c.is_alphabetic() || *c == ' ')`.
Split on whitespace, insert into `HashMap<String, usize>` using the entry API.
Collect entries into a `Vec<(String, usize)>`, sort by count descending then alphabetically on ties.
Print the top N entries as `word: count`.
Parse `--top N` from `std::env::args` and fall back to 10.
success_criteria: The entry API (`or_insert`) is used — no `contains_key` + `insert` pattern.
Output is sorted correctly: count descending, alpha ascending on ties.
Running on a 500-word input produces correct results verified by hand for the top 3 words.
`cargo clippy` is clean.
extension_ideas: Add a `--exclude stopwords.txt` flag to skip common words.
Output results as JSON using only the standard library (`serde` not allowed).

applications.title: CSV Parser with Custom Errors
applications.summary: Build real-world error handling by writing a CSV parser that propagates IO and parse errors through a custom error enum.
applications.prompt: Create a library `csvparse` that reads a CSV file with a header row, parses each subsequent row into a `Record { name: String, age: u32, score: f64 }`, and returns `Result<Vec<Record>, AppError>`. Define `AppError` as an enum with variants `Io(std::io::Error)`, `MissingField(usize)` (row number), and `ParseError { row: usize, field: String, value: String }`.
applications.deliverable: A library crate with `From<io::Error>` implemented on `AppError`, a `Display` impl on `AppError`, at least six tests (happy path, missing field, bad age, bad score, empty file, header-only file), and a small `main.rs` that demonstrates the `?` operator through a full call chain.
steps: Define `AppError` enum and implement `std::fmt::Display` on it.
Implement `From<std::io::Error> for AppError` so IO errors coerce with `?`.
Write `parse_csv(path: &str) -> Result<Vec<Record>, AppError>` using `?` throughout — no `unwrap`.
Test happy path with a two-row CSV, then test each error variant by passing malformed input.
Write a `main.rs` that calls `parse_csv`, prints each record on success, or prints the error on failure.
success_criteria: `AppError` implements `Display` and `std::error::Error`.
`From<io::Error>` is implemented — `?` converts IO errors automatically.
No `unwrap()` or `expect()` calls inside `parse_csv`.
All six tests pass.
extension_ideas: Add `impl std::error::Error for AppError` with `source()` returning the inner IO error.
Support dynamic column schemas using a header-driven field map.

applications.title: Generic Stack with Trait Bounds
applications.summary: Cement generics and traits by building a type-safe generic stack that works with any Display + Clone type and prints a human-readable state.
applications.prompt: Create a library `genstack` with `Stack<T>` where T must implement `Clone`. Add `push`, `pop() -> Option<T>`, `peek() -> Option<&T>`, `is_empty()`, `size()`, and a `display()` method (requires `T: Display`) that prints the stack top-to-bottom as `[top → ... → bottom]`. Write a generic function `transfer<T: Clone>(from: &mut Stack<T>, to: &mut Stack<T>)` that pops all items from `from` and pushes them onto `to`.
applications.deliverable: A library with at least eight tests, including generic use with both `i32` and `String`, and tests that verify the `transfer` function preserves order.
steps: Define `Stack<T>` wrapping a `Vec<T>` — the vec is private.
Implement all five methods on `impl<T: Clone> Stack<T>`.
Add `display()` in a separate `impl<T: Clone + std::fmt::Display> Stack<T>` block.
Implement `transfer` as a standalone generic function with a `where T: Clone` bound.
Write tests: push 3 items, pop 2, peek, display, transfer to empty stack, transfer preserves reverse order.
success_criteria: `Stack<T>` works with any `Clone` type — tested with `i32` and `String`.
`display()` is only available when `T: Display` (orphan rule respected).
`transfer` tests verify the stack is empty after and the target has items in correct order.
No `pub` on the internal `Vec`.
extension_ideas: Add a lifetime-annotated `iter()` method returning a slice iterator over `&T`.
Implement `Iterator` for `Stack<T>` to consume the stack lazily.

applications.title: Functional Log Analyzer
applications.summary: Replace explicit loops with idiomatic iterator chains to transform, filter, and summarize a log stream — proving zero-cost abstraction in practice.
applications.prompt: Create a binary `loganalyze` that reads a newline-delimited log file where each line is `LEVEL: message` (e.g. `ERROR: disk full`). Using only iterator adapters (no `for` loops in processing code), produce: (1) a count per level, (2) all ERROR messages with line numbers, (3) the five most recent WARN messages. Write the output to stdout.
applications.deliverable: A runnable binary where all data processing is done with `.map()`, `.filter()`, `.enumerate()`, `.fold()` or similar — reviewable by grep for absence of `for` in processing functions.
steps: Read the file into a `String`, split on newlines, enumerate to track line numbers.
Parse each line into a `LogEntry { line: usize, level: String, message: String }` using `.map()` and `.filter_map()` — skip malformed lines.
Use `.filter()` + `.count()` to build level counts, then `.collect::<HashMap<_, _>>()`.
Chain `.filter()` + `.map()` for ERROR messages with line numbers.
Use `.filter()` + `.rev()` + `.take(5)` for recent WARNs.
success_criteria: No `for` loop appears in any processing function — only iterator adapters.
Malformed lines are silently skipped with `filter_map`, not panicked on.
Output is clearly formatted with section headers.
`cargo clippy` is clean.
extension_ideas: Add a `--level WARN` flag to filter output to a single level.

applications.title: Parallel Word Counter
applications.summary: Apply threads, channels, and Arc<Mutex<T>> to build a concurrent text analysis pipeline — and observe that Rust's type system prevents the data races you'd write by accident in other languages.
applications.prompt: Build a binary `parcount` that reads a text file, splits it into N equal chunks (N = number of CPUs, default 4), spawns one thread per chunk, counts word frequencies in each thread, then merges results into a single `HashMap<String, usize>` and prints the top 20 words. Implement two versions: one using `Arc<Mutex<HashMap>>` shared state and one using channels — compare them in a README note.
applications.deliverable: A binary with two functions `count_shared` and `count_channels`, a `main` that calls both and asserts they produce identical results, and a short README note comparing the two approaches.
steps: Read the file, split into N chunks by line count using `.chunks()` on a `Vec<String>`.
Implement `count_shared`: create `Arc<Mutex<HashMap<String, usize>>>`, clone the Arc N times, spawn threads with `move` closures that lock, count, and release.
Implement `count_channels`: create one `mpsc::channel`, clone the `Sender` N times, each thread sends a local `HashMap`, the main thread drains the receiver and merges.
Assert both functions return identical results on the same input.
Print top 20 words from `count_channels` output.
success_criteria: Both `count_shared` and `count_channels` compile — the type system rejects any accidental data race.
The assertion that both produce identical results passes.
Threads are joined before results are used.
The README note identifies at least one concrete tradeoff between the two approaches.
extension_ideas: Replace manual chunking with a thread pool using the `rayon` crate.
Measure wall-clock time for both versions with `std::time::Instant` and note the difference.
